items.stem: A company places on the market a toy that is itself an AI system covered by Union harmonisation legislation on toy safety listed in Annex I of the AI Act, and that legislation requires the toy to undergo third-party conformity assessment before being placed on the market. Under Article 6(1), how must this AI system be classified?

options.text: As high-risk, because both the Annex I product condition and the third-party conformity assessment condition are fulfilled
As high-risk only if it also appears in the list of use cases in Annex III
As not high-risk, because Article 6(1) applies only to safety components and not to AI systems that are themselves products
As minimal-risk, because the third-party conformity assessment requirement is a matter of Union harmonisation legislation unrelated to AI Act risk classification

items.rationale: Article 6(1) provides an AI system is high-risk where (a) it is a safety component of a product, or is itself a product, covered by Annex I Union harmonisation legislation, AND (b) that product is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment for placing on the market. Both conditions are met, so A is correct. B wrongly imports the separate Annex III route (Article 6(2)) as a precondition for the paragraph 1 test. C is wrong because Article 6(1) expressly covers AI systems that are themselves products, not only safety components. D wrongly treats the conformity-assessment limb as extraneous when it is the second element of the Article 6(1) test itself.

items.stem: Besides the high-risk AI systems described in Article 6(1) concerning Annex I products, Article 6(2) provides a second, independent route to high-risk classification. What is that route?

options.text: AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered high-risk
AI systems that have undergone third-party conformity assessment under any Union harmonisation legislation
AI systems that a national competent authority designates as high-risk on a case-by-case basis
AI systems that process biometric data, regardless of the use case involved

items.rationale: Article 6(2) states: 'In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered to be high-risk.' A is correct. B confuses the Article 6(1) conformity-assessment criterion with a standalone trigger under paragraph 2, which does not exist. C invents an ad hoc national-authority designation power not found in Article 6. D overstates scope by treating any biometric processing as automatically high-risk, whereas Article 6 ties high-risk status to the Annex III use-case list, not to a data-category criterion.

items.stem: A provider develops an Annex III AI system that is intended only to perform a narrow procedural task and does not materially influence the outcome of decision-making. The provider wants to rely on the Article 6(3) derogation to treat the system as not high-risk. Which additional condition, stated in the last subparagraph of Article 6(3), overrides this derogation and forces high-risk status regardless of the narrow-procedural-task exemption?

options.text: The AI system performs profiling of natural persons
The AI system is deployed by a public authority rather than a private provider
The AI system's training computation exceeds 10^25 floating point operations
The AI system generates synthetic content that is not labelled as machine-generated

items.rationale: Article 6(3) states: 'Notwithstanding the first subparagraph, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall always be considered to be high-risk where the AI system performs profiling of natural persons,' which overrides the narrow-procedural-task and other listed exemptions. B invents a public/private distinction absent from Article 6(3). C is the Article 51(2) compute threshold for general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, a different classification regime entirely, not relevant to Annex III high-risk carve-outs. D is the Article 50(2) synthetic-content marking obligation, an unrelated transparency duty, not a trigger for high-risk status under Article 6.

items.stem: A provider concludes that its Annex III AI system does not pose a significant risk of harm and therefore is not high-risk under Article 6(3). Before placing the system on the market, what must the provider do under Article 6(4)?

options.text: Document that assessment and remain subject to the registration obligation set out in Article 49(2)
Obtain prior approval of the assessment from the Commission before placing the system on the market
Publish the assessment in the EU database of high-risk AI systems
Notify the deployer that no conformity assessment is required for the system

items.rationale: Article 6(4) requires the provider to 'document its assessment before that system is placed on the market or put into service' and states the provider 'shall be subject to the registration obligation set out in Article 49(2)'; documentation must be provided to national competent authorities on request. A is correct. B invents a prior-approval requirement Article 6(4) does not impose (documentation, not Commission pre-clearance). C misstates the registration duty as EU-database publication of a non-high-risk system's assessment, which the text does not require. D fabricates a deployer-notification duty not found in the paragraph.

items.stem: Under Article 6(6) and 6(7), the Commission may adopt delegated acts to amend the conditions in Article 6(3), second subparagraph (the narrow-procedural-task type exemptions). What is the key asymmetry between the Commission's power under paragraph 6 versus its duty under paragraph 7?

options.text: Paragraph 6 empowers the Commission to add or modify conditions where evidence shows an Annex III system poses no significant risk, whereas paragraph 7 obliges the Commission to delete conditions where evidence shows deletion is necessary to maintain the level of protection
Paragraph 6 obliges deletion of conditions while paragraph 7 merely empowers the Commission to add new conditions
Both paragraphs empower the Commission only to add conditions, differing solely in the consultation procedure required
Paragraph 6 applies to Annex I products while paragraph 7 applies to Annex III use cases

items.rationale: Article 6(6) provides the Commission 'is empowered to adopt delegated acts... to amend paragraph 3, second subparagraph... by adding new conditions... or by modifying them, where there is concrete and reliable evidence of the existence of AI systems that fall under the scope of Annex III, but do not pose a significant risk.' Article 6(7) provides the Commission 'shall adopt delegated acts... to amend paragraph 3, second subparagraph... by deleting any of the conditions... where there is concrete and reliable evidence that this is necessary to maintain the level of protection.' A correctly captures the add/modify (permissive) versus delete (mandatory) asymmetry. B reverses the two paragraphs' functions. C flattens the asymmetry by ignoring the mandatory deletion duty in paragraph 7. D is wrong because both paragraphs concern the Article 6(3) Annex III derogation conditions, not an Annex I/Annex III split.

items.stem: A provider deploys a chatbot intended to interact directly with natural persons. Under Article 50(1), what must the provider ensure regarding the persons interacting with it?

options.text: That the natural persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious to a reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect person given the circumstances
That the natural persons give explicit consent before every interaction with the AI system
That the AI system is registered in the EU database before it interacts with any natural person
That the outputs of the AI system are marked in a machine-readable format detectable as artificially generated

items.rationale: Article 50(1) requires providers to ensure such AI systems 'are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the point of view of a natural person who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect.' A matches exactly. B invents a consent requirement not present in Article 50(1) (the obligation is disclosure/design, not consent). C misapplies the Article 49(2) registration obligation, which concerns high-risk systems, not the Article 50(1) interaction-disclosure duty. D is the separate Article 50(2) synthetic-content marking obligation for generative outputs, not the direct-interaction disclosure duty of paragraph 1.

items.stem: Under Article 50(1), the obligation to inform natural persons that they are interacting with an AI system does not apply to AI systems authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences. What condition, stated in the same sentence, limits this exception?

options.text: The exception ceases to apply if the systems are available for the public to report a criminal offence
The exception ceases to apply once the system has been used in more than one investigation
The exception applies only to systems operated exclusively by Europol
The exception applies only for the first six months of the system's deployment

items.rationale: Article 50(1) states the law-enforcement exception applies 'subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of third parties, unless those systems are available for the public to report a criminal offence.' A is the textually stated limiting condition. B, C, and D fabricate temporal or institutional limits (usage count, Europol-only scope, a six-month window) that appear nowhere in Article 50(1).

items.stem: A provider of a general-purpose AI system uses it to generate synthetic image content. Under Article 50(2), what must the provider ensure about the outputs, and what qualifies the strength of that obligation?

options.text: That outputs are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated, with technical solutions that are effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as technically feasible
That outputs are accompanied by a signed statement from the deployer confirming human review took place
That outputs are withheld from publication until the AI Office certifies the labelling method
That outputs are encrypted so that only the deployer can verify their artificial origin

items.rationale: Article 50(2) requires providers to 'ensure that the outputs of the AI system are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated,' with technical solutions that are 'effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as this is technically feasible,' considering content type, implementation costs, and the state of the art. A matches. B confuses this with the Article 50(4) editorial-responsibility/human-review carve-out for deployers publishing AI-generated text, a distinct obligation. C invents an AI Office pre-certification gate not found in Article 50(2). D misstates 'machine-readable and detectable' marking as encryption restricted to the deployer, contradicting the public-detectability purpose of the provision.

items.stem: A deployer publishes an AI-generated text discussing a matter of public interest. Under Article 50(4), second subparagraph, in which of the following situations is the deployer nonetheless exempt from the obligation to disclose that the text was artificially generated?

options.text: Where the AI-generated content has undergone a process of human review or editorial control and a natural or legal person holds editorial responsibility for the publication
Where the text was generated using a general-purpose AI model rather than a narrow AI system
Where the deployer is a private individual rather than a media organisation
Where the text is published behind a paywall rather than freely accessible

items.rationale: Article 50(4), second subparagraph, states the disclosure obligation for AI-generated public-interest text 'shall not apply where the use is authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences or where the AI-generated content has undergone a process of human review or editorial control and where a natural or legal person holds editorial responsibility for the publication of the content.' A is the textual exemption. B, C, and D invent distinctions (model type, deployer identity, paywall status) that Article 50(4) does not use as exemption criteria.

items.stem: A developer trains a general-purpose AI model using a cumulative amount of computation, measured in floating point operations, greater than 10^25. Under Article 51(2), what is the legal consequence of this fact alone?

options.text: The model is presumed to have high impact capabilities under Article 51(1)(a), triggering classification as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk
The model is automatically classified as a high-risk AI system under Article 6(2)
The model must undergo third-party conformity assessment before being placed on the market
The model is exempt from the transparency obligations in Article 50 because of its scale

items.rationale: Article 51(2) states: 'A general-purpose AI model shall be presumed to have high impact capabilities pursuant to paragraph 1, point (a), when the cumulative amount of computation used for its training measured in floating point operations is greater than 10^25.' A is correct. B wrongly imports the Article 6(2)/Annex III high-risk-system regime, which is a distinct classification track from the Chapter V general-purpose-model-with-systemic-risk regime. C invents a conformity-assessment consequence that Article 51 does not state (Article 51 concerns classification, not conformity assessment procedure). D is a converse-scope error: nothing in Article 51 exempts high-compute models from Article 50 transparency duties; the two obligations are independent.

items.stem: Under Article 51(1)(b), besides the Article 51(1)(a) high-impact-capabilities criterion, a general-purpose AI model can also be classified as having systemic risk based on a Commission decision. What must trigger or support that Commission decision, according to the text?

options.text: A decision taken ex officio or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel, having regard to the criteria set out in Annex XIII, where the model has capabilities or impact equivalent to those in point (a)
A binding recommendation from the European Artificial Intelligence Board that must precede any Commission decision
A referral from a national market surveillance authority acting under Union harmonisation legislation
A finding that the model's compute exceeds 10^25 floating point operations, confirmed by an independent auditor

items.rationale: Article 51(1)(b) provides for classification 'based on a decision of the Commission, ex officio or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel, [where the model] has capabilities or an impact equivalent to those set out in point (a) having regard to the criteria set out in Annex XIII.' A tracks the text precisely. B fabricates a binding Board recommendation as a precondition, which the text does not require. C invents a market-surveillance-authority referral mechanism not mentioned in Article 51. D wrongly restates the point (a)/Article 51(2) compute-threshold presumption as if it were the point (b) mechanism, conflating the two distinct routes to systemic-risk classification.

items.stem: Under Article 51(3), the Commission may adopt delegated acts to amend the thresholds listed in Article 51(1) and (2). For what stated purpose, and in light of what factors, is this power to be exercised?

options.text: To supplement benchmarks and indicators and reflect the state of the art, in light of evolving technological developments such as algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency, when necessary
To harmonise the thresholds with equivalent compute limits set under Regulation (EU) 2016/679
To reduce the compute threshold annually by a fixed percentage regardless of technological change
To align the thresholds with the third-party conformity assessment requirements referred to in Article 6(1)

items.rationale: Article 51(3) empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts 'to amend the thresholds listed in paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article, as well as to supplement benchmarks and indicators in light of evolving technological developments, such as algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency, when necessary, for these thresholds to reflect the state of the art.' A matches. B invents a cross-reference to the GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679), a sibling regulation with no compute-threshold provisions at all — a named-sibling-regulation trap. C fabricates a fixed, automatic annual reduction mechanism, contradicting the text's evidence- and technology-driven, as-necessary standard. D wrongly links the Article 51 systemic-risk thresholds to the unrelated Article 6(1) conformity-assessment criterion for Annex I products.

items.stem: A mobile investment app uses AI-generated interface cues calibrated to each user's subconscious response patterns, timed to push users toward high-fee products they would not otherwise choose. Several users incur substantial financial losses they would have avoided under normal decision-making. The developer never intended these specific losses. Under Article 5(1)(a) AI Act, is deploying this system prohibited?

options.text: Yes, because the prohibition covers systems whose effect (not only their objective) materially distorts behaviour and causes or is reasonably likely to cause significant harm.
No, because Article 5(1)(a) only prohibits subliminal techniques used with the specific intent of causing harm.
No, because Article 5(1)(a) applies only to techniques exploiting a person's age, disability, or economic situation.
No, because the technique must be shown to target one specifically named individual rather than a group of users.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(a) prohibits systems used 'with the objective, or the effect' of materially distorting behaviour causing or reasonably likely to cause significant harm — the effect prong applies regardless of intent, so B's intent-only reading is a converse error. C describes the separate vulnerability-exploitation prohibition in Article 5(1)(b), a sibling-clause trap. D is wrong because the text expressly covers harm to 'that person, another person or group of persons,' so a single-target requirement overstates the scope.

items.stem: An online lender's AI system identifies applicants over 75 years old with limited digital literacy and presents loan terms in a way that leads them, more than other applicants, to accept high-interest products causing them significant financial harm. Which Article 5(1) provision most directly prohibits this practice?

options.text: Article 5(1)(b), prohibiting AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities due to age, disability, or a specific social or economic situation to materially distort behaviour causing significant harm.
Article 5(1)(a), prohibiting subliminal techniques beyond a person's consciousness that manipulate any person's decisions.
Article 5(1)(c), prohibiting social scoring based on inferred personal characteristics leading to detrimental treatment.
Article 5(1)(g), prohibiting biometric categorisation systems that infer sensitive personal attributes.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(b) specifically covers exploitation of vulnerabilities 'due to their age, disability or a specific social or economic situation.' B is wrong because Article 5(1)(a) addresses subliminal or manipulative techniques generally, without the age/disability/economic-situation trigger. C is wrong because social scoring under 5(1)(c) requires evaluation of social behaviour over time, not age-targeted loan terms. D is wrong because 5(1)(g) concerns inferring race, political opinion, etc. from biometric data, which is unrelated to this scenario.

items.stem: A municipal AI platform continuously scores residents using data from their online shopping habits. Residents with low scores are later denied an unrelated benefit, a library late-fee waiver, in a context that has nothing to do with their shopping activity. Which feature makes this scoring practice prohibited under Article 5(1)(c)?

options.text: The detrimental treatment occurs in a social context unrelated to the context in which the underlying data was originally generated or collected.
The scoring system uses AI rather than a traditional statistical model to calculate the score.
The scoring system evaluates individual residents rather than corporate entities.
The scoring system is operated by a municipal authority rather than a private company.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(c)(i) prohibits social scoring where the score leads to detrimental treatment 'in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts in which the data was originally generated or collected' — exactly the library/shopping mismatch here. B, C, and D describe features the text never conditions the prohibition on (technology type, entity type, or operator identity), making them fabricated criteria.

items.stem: A national police AI tool assigns a 'future offender risk score' to individuals based solely on a personality-trait profile derived from social media analysis, with no link to any specific criminal investigation or objective facts about a particular offence. Under Article 5(1)(d), is deploying this tool prohibited?

options.text: Yes, because it predicts the risk of a person committing a criminal offence based solely on profiling or personality-trait assessment, without supporting a human assessment grounded in objective, verifiable facts linked to criminal activity.
No, because the tool scores a population of individuals rather than identifying a single named suspect.
No, because risk-assessment tools of this kind are only prohibited when used by private companies, not by police forces.
Yes, but only because the tool processes social media data, which Article 5(1)(d) specifically prohibits AI systems from using.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(d) prohibits risk assessments predicting criminal offending 'based solely on the profiling of a natural person or on assessing their personality traits,' with an exception only for tools supporting human assessment 'already based on objective and verifiable facts directly linked to a criminal activity' — absent here. B is wrong because the text does not exempt population-level scoring. C is wrong because the prohibition is not limited by the operator's public/private status; the only carve-out concerns the evidentiary basis of the assessment. D is wrong because the source of the data is not the legal trigger — the 'solely profiling/personality traits' basis is.

items.stem: A startup builds a facial-recognition database by scraping billions of face images from public social media profiles and public CCTV live-streams, without any targeted selection criteria, and sells access to the database. Under Article 5(1)(e), is placing this database-building AI system on the market prohibited?

options.text: Yes, because Article 5(1)(e) prohibits AI systems that create or expand facial recognition databases through untargeted scraping of facial images from the internet or CCTV footage.
No, because Article 5(1)(e) only prohibits the later use of the resulting database, not the placing on the market of the system that creates it.
No, because facial recognition databases are prohibited only when built exclusively from CCTV footage, not from internet sources.
Yes, but only if the resulting database is later used for real-time remote biometric identification by law enforcement.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(e) prohibits 'the placing on the market, the putting into service for this specific purpose, or the use' of such database-creating systems, so B's use-only reading is wrong. C is wrong because the text says 'the internet or CCTV footage,' covering either source, not CCTV exclusively. D is wrong because the prohibition on creating the database is not conditioned on a subsequent law-enforcement RBI use, which is the separate matter addressed in Article 5(1)(h).

items.stem: A university deploys an AI system in lecture halls that analyzes students' facial expressions and voice tone to infer boredom or frustration during exams, so as to flag disengaged students to instructors. There is no medical or safety justification for the deployment. Under Article 5(1)(f), is this prohibited?

options.text: Yes, because it infers emotions of natural persons in an education institution, and no medical or safety exception applies.
No, because Article 5(1)(f) only prohibits emotion inference in the workplace, not in education institutions.
No, because the prohibition applies only when an emotion-inference system also deduces protected characteristics such as race or religion.
Yes, but only because the system processes voice data, which Article 5(1)(f) treats differently from facial data.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(f) prohibits inferring emotions 'in the areas of workplace and education institutions,' except for medical or safety reasons — both conditions are met here. B is wrong because the text expressly names both settings, not workplace alone. C is wrong because that description matches the separate biometric-categorisation prohibition in Article 5(1)(g), not (f). D is wrong because the text draws no distinction between facial and voice modalities.

items.stem: A retailer deploys a biometric categorisation system that analyzes customers' facial biometric data at checkout to infer their probable religious affiliation, in order to target them with tailored promotions. Which best describes the legal status of this system under Article 5(1)(g)?

options.text: Prohibited, because it categorises individuals based on biometric data to deduce or infer religious or philosophical beliefs, and no exception for retail promotion applies.
Permitted, because Article 5(1)(g) only prohibits categorisation to infer race and political opinions, not religious or philosophical beliefs.
Permitted, because the exception for labelling or filtering lawfully acquired biometric datasets covers any commercial use of biometric categorisation.
Permitted, because Article 5(1)(g) applies only to biometric categorisation systems used by law enforcement authorities.

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(g) lists race, political opinions, trade union membership, 'religious or philosophical beliefs,' sex life or sexual orientation as prohibited inferences, so B's narrower list is a sibling-attribute trap that omits religion. C overstates the actual carve-out, which covers only labelling/filtering of lawfully acquired datasets or law-enforcement categorising, not 'any commercial use.' D commits a converse error: the carve-out mentions law enforcement, but the prohibition itself is general, not limited to law-enforcement actors.

items.stem: Local police in a Member State want to deploy a 'real-time' remote biometric identification system in a public square to locate a specific missing person under circumstances suggesting she may be a trafficking victim. Under Article 5(1)(h), how is this use treated?

options.text: It falls within a listed objective (targeted search for trafficking victims or missing persons) for which the general prohibition does not apply, subject to the other safeguards in Article 5.
It is prohibited outright, because Article 5(1)(h) bans all real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces without exception.
It is permitted with no further conditions, because locating missing persons is expressly exempted from all safeguards in Article 5.
It is prohibited, because locating a missing person is not among the objectives listed in Article 5(1)(h).

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(h)(i) lists 'the targeted search for specific victims of ... trafficking in human beings ... as well as the search for missing persons' as an objective for which the use is not prohibited, 'unless and in so far as' strictly necessary. B overstates the prohibition as absolute, ignoring the listed exceptions. C wrongly assumes the exception removes all further safeguards, but paragraphs 2 and 3 still apply. D is factually wrong since this objective is expressly listed.

items.stem: A law enforcement authority in a Member State plans to deploy a 'real-time' remote biometric identification system in a train station to identify a suspect for a purpose listed under Article 5(1)(h). Which considerations does Article 5(2) require the authority to take into account before deployment?

options.text: The seriousness, probability and scale of the harm that would be caused if the system were not used, and the seriousness, probability and scale of the consequences of using it for the rights and freedoms of all persons concerned.
Only the total number of individuals whose biometric data will be scanned during the deployment.
Only whether the train station is owned by a public authority or a private company.
Only whether the suspect has previously been convicted of a similar offence.

items.rationale: Article 5(2)(a) and (b) require weighing 'the nature of the situation ... in particular the seriousness, probability and scale of the harm' if the system is not used, and the same factors for 'the consequences of the use of the system for the rights and freedoms of all persons concerned.' B, C, and D each substitute a single fabricated factor that the text does not mention as the criterion.

items.stem: A national police force wants to use a 'real-time' remote biometric identification system in a public square for an urgent terrorist-threat scenario and has not yet obtained authorisation from the competent judicial authority. Under Article 5(3), what is the maximum permitted delay before it must request that authorisation?

options.text: The authorisation must be requested without undue delay, at the latest within 24 hours.
The authorisation must be requested within 72 hours of the deployment beginning.
No authorisation is required in cases of urgency, only subsequent notification to the market surveillance authority.
The authorisation must be obtained in advance in all cases, with no urgency exception permitted.

items.rationale: Article 5(3) states that in a duly justified situation of urgency, use 'may be commenced without an authorisation provided that such authorisation is requested without undue delay, at the latest within 24 hours.' B substitutes a fabricated 72-hour figure. C confuses the authorisation requirement of paragraph 3 with the separate notification duty in paragraph 4, and wrongly removes the authorisation requirement entirely. D ignores the express urgency exception in the text.

items.stem: A Member State decides to allow its law enforcement authorities to use 'real-time' remote biometric identification systems in publicly accessible spaces, within the limits of Article 5(1)(h) and (2)-(3), and lays down national rules accordingly. Under Article 5(5), what must that Member State do regarding these rules?

options.text: Notify the detailed national rules to the Commission at the latest 30 days following their adoption.
Submit the rules to the European Data Protection Board for prior approval before they take effect.
Publish the rules in the Official Journal of the European Union within 10 days of adoption.
Obtain unanimous consent from all other Member States before the rules take effect.

items.rationale: Article 5(5) requires that 'Member States shall notify those rules to the Commission at the latest 30 days following the adoption thereof.' B, C, and D each invent a plausible-sounding EU procedural step (prior board approval, Official Journal publication, unanimous consent) that the text does not require.

items.stem: An AI system used for a marketing purpose does not fall within any of the specific prohibited practices listed in Article 5(1)(a) to (h), but its use infringes a separate EU consumer-protection regulation. Under Article 5(8), what is the effect of Article 5 on this situation?

options.text: Article 5 does not affect the prohibitions that apply under the other Union law that is infringed; that separate prohibition still applies.
Article 5 pre-empts and supersedes all other Union law prohibitions, so only Article 5 can apply to AI systems.
Article 5 automatically extends its list of prohibited practices to include any practice that infringes other Union law.
Article 5 renders the other Union law inapplicable to AI systems until the Commission issues implementing guidance.

items.rationale: Article 5(8) states 'this Article shall not affect the prohibitions that apply where an AI practice infringes other Union law,' meaning the separate consumer-protection prohibition remains fully applicable alongside Article 5. B states the exact converse of the text. C misreads 'does not affect' as an automatic incorporation of other law into the Article 5(1) list. D invents a Commission-guidance mechanism not found in the text.

items.stem: A provider is establishing a risk management system for a high-risk AI system under Article 9 of the AI Act. Which of the following best describes how this system must function over the product's lifecycle?

options.text: As a continuous iterative process run throughout the entire lifecycle, requiring regular systematic review and updating
As a one-time assessment conducted before the system is placed on the market, with no further updates required
As an annual audit conducted only after a serious incident has been reported
As a process limited to the design phase, concluding once the technical documentation is finalized

items.rationale: Art. 9(2) defines the risk management system as 'a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle... requiring regular systematic review and updating'. B is the converse error (treats it as a single pre-market event). C wrongly ties the ongoing system to the separate serious-incident reporting duty. D understates scope by confining it to the design phase only.

items.stem: Under Article 9, when evaluating 'other risks possibly arising' as part of a high-risk AI system's risk management, on what must this evaluation be based?

options.text: Data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in Article 72
Complaints submitted directly by individual data subjects
The benchmarks and measurement methodologies the Commission encourages under Article 15
Inspection reports issued by market surveillance authorities under Article 74

items.rationale: Art. 9(2)(c) requires evaluation of other risks 'based on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in Article 72'. B invents an unreferenced complaint channel. C confuses the Commission's benchmark-development role under Art. 15(2), which is unrelated to this evaluation step. D confuses the provider's own risk evaluation duty with a different actor's (market surveillance authority) activity.

items.stem: Article 9 requires providers, when implementing the risk management system, to give consideration to whether the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on which group specifically named in the article?

options.text: Persons under the age of 18
Persons with limited digital literacy
Employees of the deploying organization
Non-EU nationals subject to the system

items.rationale: Art. 9(9) names 'persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups' as the specific consideration providers must give in the risk management system. B, C and D are plausible vulnerable categories in AI ethics discourse but are not the group the article names.

items.stem: A provider of a high-risk AI system is already subject to internal risk-management requirements under other relevant Union law. According to Article 9(10), how may this provider address the AI Act's risk management requirements?

options.text: The Article 9 risk management aspects may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures already established under that other Union law
The provider is fully exempted from Article 9 because equivalent Union law already applies
The provider must run two entirely separate and independent risk management systems in parallel
The provider must obtain a derogation from the Commission before combining the two systems

items.rationale: Art. 9(10) allows the Art. 9 aspects to 'be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that law' — integration, not exemption. B overstates the effect as a full exemption. C is the converse error, requiring needless duplication the article does not demand. D invents a Commission derogation procedure not found in the text.

items.stem: Under Article 10, data governance practices for training, validation and testing data sets of a high-risk AI system must include examination for possible biases. What must this examination specifically address, per the article?

options.text: Biases likely to affect the health and safety of persons, negatively impact fundamental rights, or lead to discrimination prohibited under Union law
Biases arising exclusively from the age of the data set since its last update
Biases that could increase the AI system's development or licensing costs
Biases identified only by third-party auditors after the system is placed on the market

items.rationale: Art. 10(2)(f) requires examination of data 'in view of possible biases that are likely to affect the health and safety of persons, have a negative impact on fundamental rights or lead to discrimination prohibited under Union law'. B, C are fabricated criteria absent from the article. D wrongly relocates a pre-market data-governance duty to a post-market third-party audit.

items.stem: A provider seeks to process special categories of personal data under Article 10(5) strictly to detect and correct bias in a high-risk AI system. Which of the following conditions must be met for this processing to be lawful under that provision?

options.text: The special category data must be deleted once the bias has been corrected or its retention period ends, whichever comes first
The special category data may be freely transmitted to other parties involved in bias research
The processing is permitted regardless of whether bias detection could be achieved using synthetic or anonymised data instead
The data may be retained indefinitely provided it remains pseudonymised

items.rationale: Art. 10(5)(e) requires deletion of the special category data 'once the bias has been corrected or the personal data has reached the end of its retention period, whichever comes first'. B contradicts point (d), which prohibits transmission to other parties. C contradicts point (a), which requires that the objective could not be achieved using other data including synthetic or anonymised data. D contradicts the mandatory deletion condition; pseudonymisation alone does not permit indefinite retention.

items.stem: A provider develops a high-risk AI system that does not use techniques involving the training of AI models. Under Article 10(6), how do the data governance requirements of paragraphs 2 to 5 apply to this system?

options.text: They apply only to the testing data sets used to develop the system
They apply in full to the training, validation, and testing data sets, exactly as with trained models
They do not apply at all, since the system does not use trained AI models
They apply only to the validation data sets used prior to deployment

items.rationale: Art. 10(6): 'For the development of high-risk AI systems not using techniques involving the training of AI models, paragraphs 2 to 5 apply only to the testing data sets.' B overstates scope (wrongly includes training/validation sets). C understates scope to zero. D names the wrong data set category.

items.stem: Under Article 13, the instructions for use accompanying a high-risk AI system must state the level of accuracy against which the system has been tested and validated. This information must be provided together with which other elements referenced in the same point?

options.text: Robustness and cybersecurity, as referred to in Article 15
Data protection impact assessment results referred to in Article 35 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679
The conformity assessment procedure chosen under Article 43
The registration number assigned under Article 71

items.rationale: Art. 13(3)(b)(ii) requires disclosure of 'the level of accuracy, including its metrics, robustness and cybersecurity referred to in Article 15... against which the high-risk AI system has been tested and validated'. B is the deployer's separate duty under Art. 26(9), not an instructions-for-use element. C and D are unrelated administrative processes not tied to the accuracy disclosure requirement.

items.stem: A deployer uses a high-risk AI system falling under point 1(a) of Annex III (remote biometric identification). Before any action or decision is taken based on an identification produced by the system, what does Article 14(5) require, as a general rule?

options.text: The identification must be separately verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons with the necessary competence, training and authority
The identification must be verified by a single natural person with specialist AI training
The identification must be automatically cross-checked against a national biometric database before any human review
The identification requires sign-off from the system's original provider before the deployer may act on it

items.rationale: Art. 14(5): 'no action or decision is taken by the deployer on the basis of the identification... unless that identification has been separately verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons'. B is a common wrong-number trap (one person instead of at least two). C invents an automated cross-check not in the text. D invents a provider sign-off requirement not present in the article.

items.stem: Article 14(5) generally requires that, for the AI systems referred to in point 1(a) of Annex III, no decision be taken based solely on the system's output unless separately verified by at least two natural persons. In which context does the article state that this two-person verification requirement does not apply?

options.text: High-risk AI systems used for the purposes of law enforcement, migration, border control or asylum, where Union or national law considers the requirement disproportionate
High-risk AI systems used exclusively within private-sector employment screening
High-risk AI systems used by financial institutions subject to internal governance requirements under Union financial services law
High-risk AI systems that have undergone conformity assessment under Annex VII with notified body involvement

items.rationale: Art. 14(5), second subparagraph: the two-person requirement 'shall not apply to high-risk AI systems used for the purposes of law enforcement, migration, border control or asylum, where Union or national law considers the application of this requirement to be disproportionate'. B invents an unrelated sector. C confuses this exception with the separate financial-institution carve-out for monitoring/logging obligations under Art. 26, which does not concern this verification requirement. D invents an unrelated conformity-assessment condition.

items.stem: Article 14 requires that natural persons assigned human oversight of a high-risk AI system be enabled to remain aware of a particular tendency described in the article. What is this tendency called?

options.text: Automation bias — the tendency to automatically rely or over-rely on the system's output
Confirmation bias — the tendency to favor information confirming pre-existing beliefs
Selection bias — the tendency to systematically favor certain samples in a data set
Anchoring bias — the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information offered

items.rationale: Art. 14(4)(b) requires awareness 'of the possible tendency of automatically relying or over-relying on the output produced by a high-risk AI system (automation bias)'. B, C and D are genuine cognitive-bias concepts but are not the term the article names, testing precise recall against conflation with similar-sounding bias concepts.

items.stem: Under Article 15, high-risk AI systems that continue to learn after being placed on the market or put into service must be developed to address a specific risk relating to possibly biased outputs. What is this risk called in the article, and what must be done about it?

options.text: Feedback loops, where biased outputs influence inputs for future operations; these must be eliminated or reduced as far as possible and addressed with appropriate mitigation measures
Model drift, which must be corrected through mandatory quarterly retraining
Data poisoning, which must be prevented through cryptographic signing of all training data
Concept collapse, which must be addressed by reverting to the system's original training configuration

items.rationale: Art. 15(4) third subparagraph requires eliminating or reducing 'as far as possible the risk of possibly biased outputs influencing input for future operations (feedback loops)... duly addressed with appropriate mitigation measures'. B and D are ML concepts not named or required in the article. C misapplies 'data poisoning', a distinct AI-specific vulnerability addressed separately in Art. 15(5), with a fabricated remedy.

items.stem: Article 15(5) requires high-risk AI systems to be resilient against attempts by unauthorised third parties to alter their use, outputs or performance. Which of the following is explicitly listed among the AI-specific vulnerabilities the technical solutions should address?

options.text: Attacks trying to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning)
Attacks exploiting default administrator passwords left unchanged
Attacks targeting the AI system's cloud hosting provider's billing infrastructure
Attacks exploiting outdated TLS certificate configurations

items.rationale: Art. 15(5) third subparagraph lists 'data poisoning', model poisoning, adversarial examples/model evasion, confidentiality attacks and model flaws as the AI-specific vulnerabilities to be addressed. B, C and D are generic IT-security issues not named in the article.

items.stem: Under Article 26(6), deployers of high-risk AI systems must keep the logs automatically generated by the system, to the extent under their control, for a period appropriate to the intended purpose and for at least how long, unless otherwise provided in applicable Union or national law?

options.text: Six months
Thirty days
One year
Five years

items.rationale: Art. 26(6) requires deployers to keep such logs 'for a period appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system, of at least six months'. B, C and D are plausible retention periods drawn from other regulatory contexts but do not match the figure stated in this article.

items.stem: A law enforcement deployer intends to use a high-risk AI system for post-remote biometric identification in the framework of an investigation targeting a suspect. Under Article 26(10), if authorisation is not obtained ex ante, by when must it be requested from a judicial or administrative authority, at the latest?

options.text: Without undue delay and no later than 48 hours after the use begins
Within 24 hours after the use begins
Within 7 days after the use begins
Before the investigation is formally opened, with no exception for urgent cases

items.rationale: Art. 26(10) requires the deployer to request authorisation 'ex ante, or without undue delay and no later than 48 hours' by a judicial or administrative authority. B and C are wrong-number traps drawn from other legal deadlines. D contradicts the article's explicit allowance for ex-post authorisation within the time limit.

items.stem: A provider trains a general-purpose AI model using a cumulative amount of computation, measured in floating point operations, that exceeds a specific threshold set in the AI Act. Above what threshold is the model presumed to have high-impact capabilities under Article 51(2), triggering classification as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk?

options.text: 10^21 floating point operations
10^23 floating point operations
10^25 floating point operations
10^27 floating point operations

items.rationale: Article 51(2) states the presumption of high-impact capabilities applies when cumulative training compute is greater than 10^25 FLOP. A (10^21) and B (10^23) understate the threshold by several orders of magnitude, which would sweep in far more ordinary models than the Act intends; D (10^27) overstates it, which would exclude models the Act is designed to catch. Each is a plain order-of-magnitude error against the one figure the article actually specifies.

items.stem: A general-purpose AI model does not meet the training-compute presumption in Article 51(2), but the scientific panel has not issued any alert about it. On what basis, if any, can the Commission still classify this model as posing systemic risk under Article 51(1)(b)?

options.text: Ex officio, having regard to the criteria set out in Annex XIII
Only if the model's provider itself requests the classification
Only following a resolution of the European Parliament
Only after notification by a national competent authority

items.rationale: Article 51(1)(b) allows classification 'based on a decision of the Commission, ex officio or following a qualified alert from the scientific panel,' assessed against the Annex XIII criteria — so a scientific panel alert is one route, not the only one. B, C, and D each invent a trigger (provider self-request, Parliament resolution, national authority notification) that Article 51 does not mention; the article vests this decision in the Commission alone.

items.stem: A provider of a general-purpose AI model wants to comply with Article 53(1)(c) regarding copyright. What does this provision require the provider to do?

options.text: Put in place a policy to comply with Union copyright law, including identifying and complying with rights reservations expressed under Article 4(3) of Directive (EU) 2019/790
Obtain an individual licence from every rightsholder whose work appears in the training data
Publish the complete training dataset so that rightsholders can verify use of their works
Pay a copyright compensation levy to the AI Office

items.rationale: Article 53(1)(c) requires a policy to comply with Union copyright law, in particular identifying and honouring rights reservations under Article 4(3) of Directive (EU) 2019/790. B overstates the obligation as individual licensing, which the text does not require. C confuses this with the separate training-data-summary obligation in point (d), which calls for a summary, not full dataset publication. D fabricates a payment mechanism absent from the text.

items.stem: Before a harmonised standard covering the Article 53(1) obligations is published, how may a provider of a general-purpose AI model demonstrate compliance with those obligations, per Article 53(4)?

options.text: By relying on codes of practice within the meaning of Article 56
By self-certifying compliance to the AI Office with no supporting evidence
By waiting until the Commission adopts an implementing act before taking any compliance steps
By relying solely on informal guidance from a national competent authority

items.rationale: Article 53(4) permits providers to rely on Article 56 codes of practice to demonstrate compliance pending a harmonised standard. B mischaracterises the regime: providers who do not adhere to a code or standard must demonstrate 'alternative adequate means of compliance for assessment by the Commission,' not bare self-certification. C wrongly implies compliance can be deferred. D substitutes an informal, unlisted mechanism for the one the article actually names.

items.stem: A provider releases a general-purpose AI model under a free and open-source licence permitting access, use, modification and distribution, and publishes the model's weights, architecture information, and usage information. The model does not present systemic risk. Which Article 53(1) obligation does the provider still have to meet, under Article 53(2)?

options.text: Making publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary of the content used for training the model
Drawing up and keeping up to date the technical documentation described in Annex XI
Drawing up and making available integration documentation to downstream AI system providers under Annex XII
None of the Article 53(1) obligations, since qualifying open-source release exempts the provider entirely

items.rationale: Article 53(2) exempts qualifying open-source providers only from the obligations in points (a) and (b) — technical documentation and downstream integration information — not from points (c) and (d). The training-data summary in point (d) therefore still applies. B and C describe exactly the obligations that ARE exempted, the reverse of what the question asks. D overstates the exemption as total, which the text explicitly does not grant.

items.stem: A provider of a general-purpose AI model is established in a third country and intends to place the model on the Union market. What must it do prior to placing the model on the market, under Article 54(1)?

options.text: Appoint, by written mandate, an authorised representative established in the Union
Establish a subsidiary company within the Union to act on its behalf
Register the model directly with every Member State's national competent authority
Notify the European Parliament of its intention to enter the Union market

items.rationale: Article 54(1) requires third-country providers to appoint, by written mandate, an authorised representative established in the Union before placing the model on the market. B substitutes a heavier corporate-establishment requirement the article does not impose. C and D invent notification/registration duties (to all national authorities, or to Parliament) that appear nowhere in Article 54.

items.stem: For how long must an authorised representative keep a copy of a general-purpose AI model's Annex XI technical documentation at the disposal of the AI Office, according to Article 54(3)(b)?

options.text: For 10 years after the model has been placed on the market
For 5 years after the model has been withdrawn from the market
For 3 years from the date the written mandate was signed
Indefinitely, with no defined end point

items.rationale: Article 54(3)(b) fixes the retention period at 10 years after the general-purpose AI model has been placed on the market. B substitutes both the wrong duration and the wrong triggering event (withdrawal rather than placing on the market). C substitutes a shorter period tied to the mandate date rather than the market-placement date. D wrongly denies that any fixed period exists.

items.stem: An authorised representative of a general-purpose AI model provider has reason to consider that the provider is acting contrary to its obligations under the AI Act. What must the authorised representative do, according to Article 54(5)?

options.text: Terminate the mandate and immediately inform the AI Office of the termination and the reasons for it
Continue performing its tasks under the mandate until the AI Office orders otherwise
Notify only the national competent authority of the third country where the provider is established
Suspend the provider's access to the Union market pending a Commission investigation

items.rationale: Article 54(5) requires the authorised representative to terminate the mandate and immediately inform the AI Office of the termination and its reasons. B contradicts this by having the representative carry on regardless. C redirects the notification to the wrong body — a third country's own authority rather than the AI Office. D grants the representative a market-suspension power the article does not confer.

items.stem: A provider releases a qualifying free and open-source general-purpose AI model, with its weights, architecture, and usage information publicly available, and the obligation to appoint an authorised representative under Article 54 would otherwise not apply. Under Article 54(6), in which circumstance does the appointment obligation apply nonetheless?

options.text: When the general-purpose AI model presents systemic risk
When the provider has not yet published a training data summary under Article 53(1)(d)
When more than one downstream provider integrates the model into an AI system
When the provider is established within the Union rather than a third country

items.rationale: Article 54(6) exempts qualifying open-source providers from the Article 54 obligation 'unless the general-purpose AI models present systemic risks' — so systemic risk revives the requirement. B ties the exemption to an unrelated obligation (the training-data summary) that Article 54(6) does not reference. C invents a downstream-integrator-count trigger not found in the text. D is a distractor built on the fact that Article 54 applies only to third-country providers in the first place, making Union establishment irrelevant to this exemption clause rather than a trigger for it.

items.stem: In addition to the obligations under Articles 53 and 54, what must a provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk do under Article 55(1)(a)?

options.text: Perform model evaluation in accordance with standardised protocols and tools reflecting the state of the art, including conducting and documenting adversarial testing
Submit the model's full source code to the AI Office for pre-market approval
Obtain third-party certification from a notified body before placing the model on the market
Publish a public risk register accessible to all Union citizens

items.rationale: Article 55(1)(a) requires model evaluation with standardised, state-of-the-art protocols and tools, including documented adversarial testing to identify and mitigate systemic risks. B invents a pre-market source-code approval regime the article does not create. C imports the notified-body conformity-assessment concept used for high-risk AI systems elsewhere in the Act, not for general-purpose models with systemic risk. D invents a public-facing register; reporting under Article 55 runs to the AI Office and national competent authorities, not the public.

items.stem: A provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk becomes aware of a serious incident linked to the model. What does Article 55(1)(c) require the provider to do?

options.text: Keep track of, document, and report the incident without undue delay to the AI Office and, as appropriate, to national competent authorities
Report the incident within 72 hours exclusively to the model's affected end users
Recall the model from the Union market within 15 days of the incident
Publish a public notice of the incident within 24 hours

items.rationale: Article 55(1)(c) requires tracking, documenting, and reporting serious incidents 'without undue delay' to the AI Office and, as appropriate, national competent authorities. B imports a 72-hour deadline and a users-only notification duty resembling the GDPR personal-data-breach regime, which this article does not impose. C fabricates a fixed recall deadline not present in the text. D fabricates a public-disclosure duty; the article directs reporting to authorities, not the public.

items.stem: Under Article 56(9), if a code of practice covering the obligations in Articles 53 and 55 cannot be finalised by 2 August 2025, or the AI Office assesses it as inadequate, what may the Commission do?

options.text: Provide common rules for the implementation of those obligations by means of implementing acts, adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 98(2)
Suspend the obligations in Articles 53 and 55 until a code of practice is agreed
Delegate the drafting of binding implementing rules to the scientific panel
Extend the codes-of-practice deadline indefinitely without taking any further action

items.rationale: Article 56(9) provides that if a code cannot be finalised by 2 August 2025, or is deemed inadequate, the Commission may provide common rules by implementing acts adopted under the Article 98(2) examination procedure. B is wrong because the underlying Articles 53 and 55 obligations remain in force regardless of the code's status. C wrongly assigns rule-making power to the scientific panel, a body tied to Article 51 classification rather than implementing rules. D contradicts the article's active remedy (implementing acts) with mere inaction.

items.stem: Under the AI Act, which body is tasked with developing the Union's expertise and capabilities in the field of AI, with Member States facilitating the tasks entrusted to it?

options.text: The AI Office
The European Artificial Intelligence Board
The European Data Protection Supervisor
The national market surveillance authorities

items.rationale: Art. 64(1)-(2): the Commission develops Union expertise and capabilities in AI through the AI Office, and Member States facilitate the tasks entrusted to it. (B) The Board is the separate coordination body composed of Member State representatives (Art. 65), not the expertise-development vehicle. (C) The EDPS only participates in the Board as an observer (Art. 65(2)); it has no role under Art. 64. (D) National market surveillance authorities are designated under Art. 70 for enforcement, not for building Union-level AI expertise.

items.stem: A Member State proposes to send two delegates to the European Artificial Intelligence Board to strengthen its influence on votes. Under Article 65, how many representatives does each Member State have on the Board?

options.text: One representative per Member State
Two representatives per Member State
One representative per Member State plus one non-voting industry delegate
A number of representatives proportional to the Member State's population

items.rationale: Art. 65(2): the Board 'shall be composed of one representative per Member State.' (B) doubles the fixed allocation, which the article does not permit. (C) invents an industry delegate not mentioned in Art. 65(2) — the only non-voting attendees named are the EDPS (observer) and the AI Office. (D) wrongly imports a population-weighting mechanism the article does not use; representation is per Member State, not proportional.

items.stem: At a meeting of the European Artificial Intelligence Board, a representative notes that the European Data Protection Supervisor is present alongside the AI Office. Under Article 65, what is the EDPS's role at Board meetings?

options.text: It participates as an observer
It chairs the Board
It casts a vote as an additional Board member
It provides the Board's secretariat

items.rationale: Art. 65(2): 'The European Data Protection Supervisor shall participate as observer.' (B) is wrong because Art. 65(8) states the Board is chaired by one of the Member State representatives, not the EDPS. (C) is wrong because observers, by definition, do not take part in votes — indeed the article specifies even the AI Office attends 'without taking part in the votes,' and the EDPS is not described as a voting member at all. (D) is wrong because Art. 65(8) assigns the secretariat function to the AI Office, not the EDPS.

items.stem: Article 65(6) requires the Board to establish two standing sub-groups. Under that provision, in what capacity does the standing sub-group for market surveillance act with respect to Regulation (EU) 2019/1020?

options.text: As the administrative cooperation group (ADCO) for the AI Act within the meaning of Article 30 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020
As the single point of contact designated under that Regulation
As a notifying authority for conformity assessment bodies under that Regulation
As the secretariat for the European Data Protection Board under that Regulation

items.rationale: Art. 65(6): 'The standing sub-group for market surveillance should act as the administrative cooperation group (ADCO) for this Regulation within the meaning of Article 30 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.' (B) confuses this sub-group with the single point of contact function, which under Art. 70(2) is a national market surveillance authority designated by each Member State, not the Board's sub-group. (C) misassigns a notifying-authority role, which under the AI Act belongs to national notifying authorities (Art. 70(1)), not the ADCO sub-group. (D) fabricates a link to the European Data Protection Board, which does not appear anywhere in Art. 65.

items.stem: A Member State designates its representative to the European Artificial Intelligence Board in 2026. Under Article 65(3), for how long is that designation made, and may it be renewed?

options.text: Three years, renewable once
Five years, renewable once
Three years, not renewable
An indefinite term, subject to annual review

items.rationale: Art. 65(3): 'Each representative shall be designated by their Member State for a period of three years, renewable once.' (B) substitutes a plausible-sounding but incorrect five-year term. (C) drops the renewal option the article explicitly grants. (D) invents an indefinite mandate with annual review, which contradicts the fixed three-year term set out in the article.

items.stem: A Member State currently has a single agency that both notifies conformity assessment bodies and carries out market surveillance under the AI Act, rather than using two separate agencies. Under Article 70(1), is this arrangement permitted?

options.text: Yes, provided the principles of independence, impartiality and objectivity are observed, since these tasks may be performed by one or more designated authorities
No, the notifying authority and the market surveillance authority must always be two legally separate bodies
No, only the AI Office may combine notifying and market surveillance functions
Yes, but only if the Member State's AI systems are all limited-risk

items.rationale: Art. 70(1): each Member State must designate at least one notifying authority and at least one market surveillance authority, but 'provided that those principles are observed, such activities and tasks may be performed by one or more designated authorities, in accordance with the organisational needs of the Member State.' (B) states the opposite of what the article permits. (C) wrongly transfers a national-level organisational option to the AI Office, which has no such role under Art. 70. (D) invents a risk-tier condition that Art. 70(1) does not contain — the combination option is general, not limited to any risk category.

items.stem: Under Article 70(2), which type of authority does a Member State designate to act as the single point of contact for the AI Act, and by what date must the public be able to find electronic contact information for competent authorities and single points of contact?

options.text: A market surveillance authority, with the contact information public by 2 August 2025
The notifying authority, with the contact information public by 2 August 2026
The national data protection authority, with the contact information public immediately upon entry into force of the Regulation
The AI Office, with the contact information public by 2 August 2025

items.rationale: Art. 70(2): 'Member States shall designate a market surveillance authority to act as the single point of contact for this Regulation,' and must make contact information for competent authorities and single points of contact publicly available electronically 'by 2 August 2025.' (B) swaps in the notifying authority and pushes the deadline a year later than stated. (C) substitutes the data protection authority, which Art. 70 never names for this function, and gives a false 'immediately' deadline. (D) wrongly assigns the single-point-of-contact role to the AI Office, which is an EU-level body under Art. 64, not a national authority Member States 'designate.'

items.stem: An AI system falling within the scope of the AI Act is developed and deployed by a Union institution rather than by a private or national operator. Under Article 70(9), which body acts as the competent authority for supervising that Union institution?

options.text: The European Data Protection Supervisor
The European Artificial Intelligence Board
The AI Office
The national market surveillance authority of the Member State hosting the institution

items.rationale: Art. 70(9): 'Where Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies fall within the scope of this Regulation, the European Data Protection Supervisor shall act as the competent authority for their supervision.' (B) confuses this supervisory role with the Board's coordination function under Art. 65, which has no supervisory competence over individual operators. (C) wrongly extends the AI Office's expertise-building role (Art. 64) into direct supervision, which the article assigns specifically to the EDPS. (D) incorrectly applies the ordinary national-authority model of Art. 70(1)-(2), which governs operators generally, not EU institutions, bodies, offices or agencies, which are carved out to the EDPS.

items.stem: A large undertaking deploys an AI system that engages in a prohibited practice under Article 5. A separate operator fails to comply with the transparency obligations for providers and deployers under Article 50. Under Article 99, how do the maximum administrative fines for these two infringements compare?

options.text: The Article 5 infringement carries a maximum of EUR 35 000 000 or 7 % of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher, while the Article 50 infringement carries a maximum of EUR 15 000 000 or 3 % of turnover, whichever is higher
Both infringements carry the same maximum of EUR 15 000 000 or 3 % of turnover, whichever is higher, because both are treated as operator obligations

The Article 5 infringement carries a maximum of EUR 15 000 000 or 3 % of turnover, whichever is higher, while the Article 50 infringement carries EUR 35 000 000 or 7 %, since transparency is the Regulation's cornerstone duty
Both infringements are capped at a flat EUR 7 500 000 regardless of turnover, since Article 99(5) covers all information-related duties

items.rationale: Art. 99(3): non-compliance with the Art. 5 prohibitions is subject to fines of up to EUR 35 000 000 or 7 % of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. Art. 99(4)(g): non-compliance with the Art. 50 transparency obligations falls under the lower tier of up to EUR 15 000 000 or 3 % of turnover, whichever is higher. (B) collapses both into the Art. 99(4) tier, ignoring that Art. 5 prohibitions are singled out for the highest tier. (C) reverses the two tiers, wrongly treating transparency as more severe than a prohibited practice. (D) misapplies Art. 99(5), which is a distinct, lower tier (up to EUR 7 500 000 or 1 % of turnover) reserved for supplying incorrect, incomplete or misleading information to notified bodies or national competent authorities, not for Art. 5 or Art. 50 infringements themselves.

items.stem: A start-up qualifying as an SME engages in a prohibited AI practice under Article 5, for which the fine would ordinarily be up to EUR 35 000 000 or 7 % of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. Under Article 99(6), how is the fine determined for this SME?

options.text: The fine is capped at whichever of the percentage or the fixed amount is lower
The fine is capped at whichever of the percentage or the fixed amount is higher, exactly as for large undertakings
SMEs, including start-ups, are exempt from administrative fines under the AI Act
The fine is fixed at a flat EUR 7 500 000 regardless of turnover, as under Article 99(5)

items.rationale: Art. 99(6): 'In the case of SMEs, including start-ups, each fine referred to in this Article shall be up to the percentages or amount referred to in paragraphs 3, 4 and 5, whichever thereof is lower.' (B) applies the general 'whichever is higher' rule from paragraphs 3-5, which is precisely what Art. 99(6) reverses for SMEs. (C) overstates the protection — Art. 99(1) and (6) show SMEs remain subject to fines, only the calculation basis changes, not the fact of liability. (D) wrongly imports the flat amount from the separate Art. 99(5) tier (incorrect/incomplete/misleading information to notified bodies or authorities), which is unrelated to the SME cap rule and unrelated to an Article 5 infringement.

items.stem: A company builds a resume-screening AI model and instructs its data-collection team to gather as much publicly available data as possible on candidates to maximize model accuracy, without assessing which fields the model actually uses. Under Article 5(1) GDPR, this approach violates which principle?

options.text: Data minimisation, because the data collected is not limited to what is necessary for the specified purpose
Storage limitation, because the data will be kept for an indefinite period
Purpose limitation, because the data was collected for scientific research
Integrity and confidentiality, because the data lacks adequate security measures

items.rationale: Article 5(1)(c) requires personal data to be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary for the purpose; collecting everything available regardless of relevance to the model breaches this. (B) storage limitation concerns retention duration, not the breadth of what is collected at intake. (C) purpose limitation concerns incompatible further use and the scientific-research carve-out, neither at issue in a commercial screening scrape. (D) integrity/confidentiality concerns security safeguards, not scope of collection.

items.stem: A hospital collected patient records for treatment under Article 6(1)(e) as a public-interest task. It now wants to reuse the dataset, without new consent or a new legal basis, to train a diagnostic AI model for a private vendor. Under Article 6(4), which factor must the controller take into account to assess whether this further processing is compatible with the original purpose?

options.text: The existence of appropriate safeguards, such as encryption or pseudonymisation, applied to the further processing
Whether the AI vendor is established within the European Economic Area
Whether the resulting model achieves an accuracy rate that satisfies market standards
Whether the training process was reviewed by an ethics committee external to the controller

items.rationale: Article 6(4)(e) lists the existence of appropriate safeguards, including encryption or pseudonymisation, as a compatibility factor. (B) the vendor's territorial establishment is a transfer-chapter concern, not a listed Article 6(4) factor. (C) model accuracy is not a GDPR compatibility criterion. (D) external ethics review is good practice but is not among the factors Article 6(4) lists.

items.stem: An AI company scrapes publicly posted social media content to build a large language model training corpus, relying on Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interests rather than seeking consent. Under Article 6(1)(f), this basis is lawful only if

options.text: the company's interest in training the model is not overridden by the data subjects' interests or fundamental rights and freedoms, particularly where the data subject is a child
the data was manifestly made public by the data subject before the scraping occurred
the company obtains explicit consent from a representative sample of the data subjects
the scraping activity was authorised by a Union legislative act specifically permitting AI training

items.rationale: Article 6(1)(f) requires that the controller's legitimate interest not be overridden by the data subject's interests or fundamental rights, with express mention of children's data. (B) restates the Article 9(2)(e) special-category exception for manifestly public data, a different article. (C) confuses this with the Article 6(1)(a) consent basis, which is not part of the legitimate-interests test. (D) confuses (f) with the legal-basis-in-law requirement applicable to points (c) and (e) of Article 6(1).

items.stem: A vendor trains a facial-recognition system using photographs collected specifically to extract facial geometry vectors that uniquely identify each individual. Under Article 9(1), this processing of biometric data for the purpose of unique identification is

options.text: prohibited unless one of the exceptions listed in Article 9(2) applies
permitted by default because photographs are not classified as special category data
permitted because the data minimisation principle in Article 5 is satisfied
prohibited only when the individuals concerned are children

items.rationale: Article 9(1) expressly prohibits processing 'biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person,' subject only to the Article 9(2) exceptions. (B) wrongly assumes photographs can never be special category data; here they are processed precisely to generate unique-identification biometric data. (C) conflates the general Article 5 minimisation principle with the categorical Article 9 prohibition, which minimisation does not cure. (D) invents an age qualifier absent from Article 9(1).

items.stem: A health-tech company wants to train an AI diagnostic model on patients' health data, a special category under Article 9(1), and seeks to rely on the Article 9(2)(a) exception. Which condition must be satisfied?

options.text: The data subject has given explicit consent to the processing for one or more specified purposes, unless Union or Member State law bars removing the prohibition even by consent
The data subject has given ordinary consent as described in Article 6(1)(a)
The processing is necessary for the performance of a contract with the data subject
The data has been anonymised prior to model training

items.rationale: Article 9(2)(a) requires explicit consent, with the caveat that Union or Member State law may prevent the prohibition being lifted even by consent. (B) substitutes the weaker ordinary-consent standard of Article 6(1)(a) for the explicit-consent standard Article 9(2)(a) actually requires. (C) imports the Article 6(1)(b) contract-necessity ground, which is not one of the Article 9(2) exceptions. (D) mischaracterises the exception: anonymised data falls outside the Regulation entirely rather than satisfying Article 9(2)(a).

items.stem: An online lender uses a machine-learning model to approve or reject loan applications with no human involved in individual decisions; rejected applicants lose access to credit entirely. This process engages the right in Article 22(1) because it

options.text: produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects the data subject and is based solely on automated processing, including profiling
involves the processing of special categories of personal data under Article 9
was not preceded by a data protection impact assessment under Article 35
relies on a training dataset collected without the data subject's knowledge

items.rationale: Article 22(1) is triggered by a decision based solely on automated processing that produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects the data subject, exactly this scenario. (B) confuses the basic Article 22(1) trigger with the separate, additional condition on special-category data in Article 22(4).
