(C) wrongly treats the Article 35 DPIA duty, a distinct upstream obligation, as the trigger for Article 22(1). (D) points to a transparency/fair-collection issue under Article 5(1)(a), not the Article 22(1) trigger.

items.stem: A subscription service uses a solely automated algorithm at signup to decide whether to accept an applicant based on predicted payment risk; rejection means no contract is formed. Under Article 22(2)(a), this automated decision falls outside the Article 22(1) prohibition because it is

options.text: necessary for entering into a contract between the data subject and the controller
based on the data subject's explicit consent to a privacy notice presented at signup
authorised by a Member State law specifically permitting credit scoring
subject to a data protection impact assessment carried out beforehand

items.rationale: Article 22(2)(a) exempts decisions necessary for entering into or performing a contract between the data subject and the controller, matching the facts exactly. (B) confuses this with the separate Article 22(2)(c) consent ground, and a privacy notice is not explicit consent in any event. (C) invokes the Article 22(2)(b) legal-authorisation ground, which is not what the facts describe. (D) treats a DPIA, an Article 35 obligation, as if it were itself one of the Article 22(2) exceptions, which it is not.

items.stem: A university relies on Article 22(2)(a) to justify a solely automated admissions-scoring algorithm, treating it as necessary for entering into the education-services contract with applicants. Under Article 22(3), what must the university additionally implement?

options.text: Suitable measures including at least the right to obtain human intervention, to express a point of view, and to contest the decision
A requirement that a human always makes the final decision instead of the algorithm
Explicit consent from each applicant prior to any automated scoring
A public register listing every automated decision made about each applicant

items.rationale: Article 22(3) requires suitable measures for decisions falling under points (a) and (c) of Article 22(2), at least the right to obtain human intervention, express a view, and contest the decision. (B) overstates the duty as replacing automation entirely, rather than guaranteeing a right to request intervention. (C) imports the separate Article 22(2)(c) consent ground, not a requirement alongside (a). (D) is not found anywhere in the text.

items.stem: A recruiting platform's automated hiring-rejection algorithm uses inferred data on applicants' religious affiliation, a special category under Article 9(1), as a scoring input, and the platform relies on Article 22(2)(a) contract necessity as the basis for the automated decision itself. Under Article 22(4), this configuration is

options.text: not permitted, because decisions under Article 22(2) may not be based on Article 9(1) special categories unless Article 9(2)(a) or (g) applies with suitable safeguards in place
permitted, because Article 22(2)(a) contract necessity independently authorises the use of any category of personal data
permitted, because the special category data is only one input among several rather than the sole basis for rejection
not permitted, because Article 22 categorically bans all use of profiling in employment decisions

items.rationale: Article 22(4) permits decisions under Article 22(2) to be based on Article 9(1) special categories only where Article 9(2)(a) explicit consent or 9(2)(g) substantial public interest applies, together with suitable safeguards; contract necessity under 22(2)(a) does not satisfy this separate condition. (B) wrongly treats the Article 22(2) exception as also curing the distinct Article 22(4) restriction. (C) invents a 'mere input' carve-out that Article 22(4) does not contain. (D) overstates the provision, which conditions rather than categorically bans profiling with legal or similarly significant effects.

items.stem: An engineering team designing a new AI recommendation system must select technical measures at the design stage. Under Article 25(1), the controller is required to implement measures, such as pseudonymisation, at which point(s) in the processing lifecycle?

options.text: Both at the time of determining the means of processing and at the time of the processing itself
Only after the AI model has been deployed into production and is generating live outputs
Only when a personal data breach affecting the training data has occurred
Only where an approved certification mechanism under Article 42 has been obtained

items.rationale: Article 25(1) requires measures both at the time of determining the means of processing and at the time of the processing itself. (B) reverses the design-stage emphasis that gives Article 25 its 'by design' character. (C) confuses this duty with breach-notification obligations, which sit elsewhere in the Regulation. (D) misreads Article 25(3), where certification is only an optional element to demonstrate compliance, not a precondition for the Article 25(1) duty.

items.stem: A voice-assistant AI is configured so that, unless a user actively changes the settings, the system records and retains full audio transcripts of all household conversations, not just activation commands, for indefinite model retraining. This default configuration is best assessed against which requirement?

options.text: Article 25(2), because by default only the personal data necessary for each specific purpose should be processed
Article 9(1), because audio recordings are inherently a prohibited special category of data
Article 6(1)(a), because consent must always be the legal basis for default data collection
Article 35(1), because carrying out a data protection impact assessment automatically renders default over-collection lawful

items.rationale: Article 25(2) requires that, by default, only personal data necessary for each specific purpose be processed, covering the amount collected, extent of processing, and storage period. (B) overstates scope: audio recordings are not automatically a special category under Article 9(1) absent content revealing such data. (C) wrongly elevates consent to a mandatory default basis, when Article 6(1) lists several alternative bases. (D) is a converse error: a DPIA under Article 35 assesses and mitigates risk, it does not legalise unlawful default over-collection.

items.stem: A bank plans to deploy an AI system that systematically and extensively evaluates customers' financial behaviour through automated profiling, whose output directly determines credit decisions with legal effects on customers. Under Article 35(3)(a), this deployment

options.text: is a case in which a data protection impact assessment shall in particular be required
is exempt from a data protection impact assessment because Article 22 already gives the data subject a right to contest the decision
requires a data protection impact assessment only if the bank also processes special categories of data under Article 9
requires a data protection impact assessment only after the supervisory authority publishes a list naming this specific processing type

items.rationale: Article 35(3)(a) names systematic and extensive evaluation based on automated processing, including profiling, on which legally or similarly significantly affecting decisions are based, as a case in particular requiring a DPIA. (B) is a converse error: Article 22 safeguards address the decision itself and do not substitute for the separate Article 35 DPIA duty. (C) wrongly merges this ground with the independent large-scale special-category ground in Article 35(3)(b); satisfying one ground does not require the other. (D) confuses the supplementary list mechanism in Article 35(4) with the direct statutory trigger in Article 35(3)(a), which applies without needing prior listing.

items.stem: A manufacturer designs a connected home thermostat. Under Article 3(1) of the Data Act, in what manner must the product data the device generates be made accessible to the user by default?

options.text: Easily, securely and free of charge, in a comprehensive, structured, commonly used and machine-readable format, and directly accessible where relevant and technically feasible
Only after the user submits a written request to the manufacturer, since default access is not required under Article 3
Free of charge, but in a proprietary format of the manufacturer's choosing for security reasons
At a reasonable fee set by the manufacturer, in a structured format

items.rationale: Article 3(1) requires that product data, by default, be 'easily, securely, free of charge, in a comprehensive, structured, commonly used and machine-readable format, and, where relevant and technically feasible, directly accessible to the user.' (B) wrongly imports the 'simple request' mechanism that Article 4(1) uses for the different scenario where direct access is NOT possible, contradicting the 'by default' design obligation of Article 3(1). (C) contradicts the express 'commonly used and machine-readable format' requirement. (D) contradicts the express 'free of charge' requirement.

items.stem: A manufacturer sells a connected product and separately offers a paid related service (a cloud analytics subscription) for that product. Before the user concludes the contract for the related service, Article 3(3) requires the prospective data holder to disclose an item that Article 3(2) does not require a seller of the connected product itself to disclose. Which item is it?

options.text: The user's right to lodge a complaint alleging an infringement of the Chapter with the competent authority designated pursuant to Article 37
The type, format and estimated volume of product data the connected product is capable of generating
Whether the connected product can store data on-device or on a remote server, and the intended duration of retention
How the user may access, retrieve or erase the data, including the technical means to do so

items.rationale: Article 3(3)(g) uniquely requires disclosure of 'the user's right to lodge a complaint alleging an infringement of any of the provisions of this Chapter with the competent authority designated pursuant to Article 37' — this item has no counterpart in the Article 3(2) list for sellers/rentors/lessors. (B), (C) and (D) are, respectively, Article 3(2)(a), 3(2)(c) and 3(2)(d) — pre-contract disclosures already owed by the seller/rentor/lessor of the connected product, so they do not distinguish the related-service provider's Article 3(3) duty.

items.stem: A user cannot directly access product data from her connected product. Under Article 4(1), on what basis and to what standard must the data holder make readily available data accessible to her?

options.text: Without undue delay, of the same quality as is available to the data holder, free of charge and in a machine-readable format, generally on the basis of a simple request through electronic means
Within 30 calendar days of the request, subject to a reasonable administrative fee to cover retrieval costs
Only after the user demonstrates a legitimate interest to the competent authority designated under Article 37
In a format chosen at the data holder's discretion, provided it is delivered within a reasonable time

items.rationale: Article 4(1) requires data holders to make readily available data accessible 'without undue delay, of the same quality as is available to the data holder... free of charge, in a comprehensive, structured, commonly used and machine-readable format... This shall be done on the basis of a simple request through electronic means where technically feasible.' (B) wrongly imports the 30-calendar-day transitional period figure from the Article 25 switching provisions and adds a fee, contradicting 'free of charge.' (C) invents a prior-approval procedure before the competent authority that Article 4(1) does not impose. (D) contradicts the mandated 'machine-readable format' and 'same quality' standards.

items.stem: A user and a data holder want to include a contract clause restricting the user's ability to access certain connected-product data. Under Article 4(2), such a restriction is permissible only where the processing of that data could:

options.text: Undermine security requirements of the connected product laid down by Union or national law, resulting in a serious adverse effect on the health, safety or security of natural persons
Reduce the manufacturer's competitive advantage in the market for related services
Reveal information that the data holder considers a trade secret and wishes to keep confidential from all parties
Increase the data holder's costs of maintaining the data infrastructure

items.rationale: Article 4(2) allows contractual restriction or prohibition only 'if such processing could undermine security requirements of the connected product, as laid down by Union or national law, resulting in a serious adverse effect on the health, safety or security of natural persons.' (B) and (D) describe commercial/cost concerns that are not grounds recognised anywhere in Article 4(2). (C) confuses this ground with the separate trade-secret withholding mechanism of Article 4(6)-(8), which requires agreed protective measures and case-by-case justification, not a blanket contractual restriction.

items.stem: A user has obtained product data from her connected product pursuant to a request under Article 4(1). Under Article 4(10), which use of that data is prohibited?

options.text: Using the data to develop a connected product that competes with the connected product from which the data originated
Sharing the data with a dispute settlement body in connection with a complaint referred under Article 4(9)
Requesting that the data holder correct inaccuracies in the metadata supplied with the data
Retaining the data on-device for longer than the retention period disclosed under Article 3(2)

items.rationale: Article 4(10) states the user 'shall not use the data obtained pursuant to a request... to develop a connected product that competes with the connected product from which the data originate, nor share the data with a third party with that intent.' (B), (C) and (D) describe conduct not addressed or prohibited by paragraph 10 of Article 4 as read.

items.stem: A data holder has obtained readily available non-personal data generated by a user's connected product. Under Article 4(13) and (14), what is the data holder permitted to do with that data?

options.text: Use it only on the basis of a contract with the user, and not make it available to third parties for commercial or non-commercial purposes beyond fulfilling that contract
Share it freely with any third party, provided the data is first anonymised
Use it to derive insights about the user's economic situation and assets, provided this use is disclosed in the pre-contractual information under Article 3(3)
Use it without a contract with the user, as long as the use does not undermine the user's commercial position

items.rationale: Article 4(13) provides the data holder 'shall only use any readily available data that is non-personal data on the basis of a contract with the user' and shall not derive insights that could undermine the user's commercial position; Article 4(14) adds that data holders 'shall not make available non-personal product data to third parties for commercial or non-commercial purposes other than the fulfilment of their contract with the user.' (B) invents an anonymisation exception the text does not contain. (C) is wrong because Article 4(13) prohibits deriving such insights outright — prior disclosure under Article 3(3) does not cure the prohibition. (D) contradicts the express requirement of a contractual basis.

items.stem: A user, having obtained data under Article 4(1), wishes to share it with an undertaking designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3 of Regulation (EU) 2022/1925. Under Article 5(3) of the Data Act, that gatekeeper:

options.text: Is not an eligible third party and shall not receive the data, nor solicit or commercially incentivise the user to obtain or share it
May receive the data only if it agrees to submit any related dispute to a dispute settlement body under Article 10(1)
May receive the data if it compensates the user with a fee for providing it
May receive the data provided it does not use it to derive commercial insights about the user

items.rationale: Article 5(3) provides that a gatekeeper 'shall not be an eligible third party under this Article and therefore shall not... solicit or commercially incentivise a user... to make data available to one of its services... nor... receive data from a user that the user has obtained pursuant to a request under Article 4(1).' (B) invents a dispute-settlement workaround not found in paragraph 3. (C) is squarely the conduct prohibited by point (a) — monetary incentivisation does not create an exception. (D) confuses the gatekeeper exclusion with the separate, unrelated restriction in Article 4(13)/5(6) on a data holder's own use of data, which is not a carve-out permitting gatekeeper receipt.

items.stem: A customer of a cloud storage service wants to move its data to a different provider. Under Article 23, providers of data processing services must remove obstacles that inhibit the customer from doing which of the following?

options.text: Porting the customer's exportable data and digital assets to a different provider or to an on-premises ICT infrastructure, including after benefiting from a free-tier offering
Obtaining a full refund of subscription fees already paid before the switching process begins
Retaining exclusive rights to analytics the source provider generated using its own proprietary algorithms
Extending the original contract indefinitely at no additional switching charge

items.rationale: Article 23(c) requires removal of obstacles inhibiting customers from 'porting the customer's exportable data and digital assets, to a different provider of data processing services or to an on-premises ICT infrastructure, including after having benefited from a free-tier offering.' (B), (C) and (D) describe matters — refunds, ownership of the provider's own derived analytics, and indefinite contract extension — that Article 23's list of switching obstacles does not address.

items.stem: A customer notifies its data processing service provider of a decision to switch to a different provider. Under Article 25, which combination correctly states the maximum notice period for initiating switching, the standard mandatory transitional period, and the minimum data retrieval period?

options.text: Notice period: two months; transitional period: 30 calendar days; retrieval period: at least 30 calendar days
Notice period: 30 calendar days; transitional period: two months; retrieval period: seven months
Notice period: seven months; transitional period: two months; retrieval period: 30 calendar days
Notice period: two months; transitional period: seven months; retrieval period: 14 working days

items.rationale: Article 25(2)(d) sets 'a maximum notice period for initiation of the switching process, which shall not exceed two months'; Article 25(2)(a) sets the 'mandatory maximum transitional period of 30 calendar days'; Article 25(2)(g) sets 'a minimum period for data retrieval of at least 30 calendar days.' (B) and (C) scramble these three figures against each other. (D) correctly states the notice period but substitutes the seven-month alternative transitional period (available only under Article 25(4) when the 30-day period is technically unfeasible) for the standard transitional period, and substitutes the unrelated 14-working-day notification deadline of Article 25(4) for the retrieval period.

items.stem: A data processing service provider determines that completing a customer's switching process within the standard 30-calendar-day transitional period is technically unfeasible. Under Article 25(4), what must the provider do?

options.text: Notify the customer within 14 working days of the switching request, duly justify the technical unfeasibility, and indicate an alternative transitional period not exceeding seven months
Terminate the contract immediately and erase the customer's exportable data without further notice
Unilaterally extend the transitional period to two months without providing any justification
Refer the matter to the competent authority designated under Article 37 for a binding ruling on technical feasibility

items.rationale: Article 25(4) requires that '[w]here the mandatory maximum transitional period... is technically unfeasible, the provider... shall notify the customer within 14 working days of the making of the switching request, and shall duly justify the technical unfeasibility and indicate an alternative transitional period, which shall not exceed seven months.' (B) contradicts the continuity obligations of Article 25(1)-(2). (C) both invents an unjustified extension and misstates the figure — two months is the separate notice-period ceiling under Article 25(2)(d), not the alternative transitional period. (D) wrongly extends the Article 37 competent-authority complaint mechanism, used for Chapter II data-access disputes, to this switching-feasibility notification, which the text does not require.

items.stem: A Member State's public-service broadcaster holds data used in fulfilling its public service broadcasting remit. A market research firm asks to re-use this data under the Chapter II regime for re-use of protected public sector data (Data Governance Act). Which statement correctly describes whether Chapter II applies, and why?

options.text: Chapter II does not apply, because data held by public service broadcasters and their subsidiaries for fulfilment of a public service broadcasting remit is excluded from Chapter II's scope.
Chapter II applies, because data protected on grounds of commercial confidentiality always falls within Chapter II regardless of the holder.
Chapter II does not apply, because the data is protected for reasons of national security.
Chapter II applies only if the broadcaster is classified as a public undertaking under national law.

items.rationale: Article 3(2)(b) excludes data held by public service broadcasters and their subsidiaries (and other bodies) for fulfilment of a public service broadcasting remit, independent of any confidentiality category under 3(1). B wrongly assumes the 3(1) confidentiality categories override the holder-based exclusions in 3(2). C reaches the right outcome but for the wrong reason: national/public security is a separate, independent exclusion (3(2)(d)) that is not the basis for excluding broadcaster data. D wrongly conflates the distinct 'public undertakings' exclusion (3(2)(a)) with the broadcaster exclusion (3(2)(b)); a broadcaster need not be a public undertaking for its exclusion to apply.

items.stem: A public sector body holds a dataset containing a private company's manufacturing process details that the company treats as a trade secret. Under Article 3(1) of the Data Governance Act, under which ground is this dataset protected for the purposes of Chapter II?

options.text: Commercial confidentiality, including business, professional and company secrets.
Statistical confidentiality.
Protection of intellectual property rights of third parties.
Protection of personal data, insofar as it falls outside the scope of Directive (EU) 2019/1024.

items.rationale: Article 3(1)(a) explicitly places trade secrets under commercial confidentiality, alongside business, professional and company secrets. C is a plausible distractor because trade secrets are IP-adjacent, but the Regulation lists 'protection of intellectual property rights of third parties' as a separate ground (3(1)(c)) distinct from commercial confidentiality. B and D name unrelated grounds (statistical data and personal data) that do not fit a manufacturing trade secret.

items.stem: A public sector body sells specialised data-analytics services to private firms as a commercial venture that, under national law, falls outside the scope of the body's public task, with that scope being transparent and subject to review. A firm requests re-use, under Chapter II, of the data underlying this venture. Which statement correctly explains whether Chapter II applies?

options.text: Chapter II does not apply, because the data is supplied in an activity falling outside the scope of the body's public task as defined by law, and that scope is transparent and subject to review.
Chapter II applies, because Chapter II governs all data held by public sector bodies irrespective of the activity to which the data relates.
Chapter II does not apply, because the venture makes the body a 'public undertaking,' which is excluded under Article 3(2)(a).
Chapter II does not apply, because the data relates to a cultural or educational establishment's activities.

items.rationale: Article 3(2)(e) excludes data whose supply is an activity falling outside the scope of the public sector body's public task, as defined by law or binding rules, provided that scope is transparent and reviewable — exactly this scenario. B overstates Chapter II's reach by ignoring this carve-out. C conflates two distinct exclusions: the body itself supplying data outside its public task (3(2)(e)) is not the same as the body being a separate 'public undertaking' (3(2)(a)). D cites an irrelevant sibling exclusion (3(2)(c), cultural/educational establishments) that does not describe this scenario.

items.stem: Under Article 5(1) of the Data Governance Act, what must a public sector body that is competent to grant or refuse access for re-use of Article 3(1) data do with respect to the conditions for re-use?

options.text: Make the conditions for allowing re-use, and the procedure for requesting re-use, publicly available via the single information point referred to in Article 8.
Negotiate and agree the conditions individually and confidentially with each prospective re-user.
Submit the conditions to the European Commission for prior approval before they take effect.
Publish the conditions only after receiving a specific request for re-use.

items.rationale: Article 5(1) requires public sector bodies to make the conditions for re-use and the request procedure publicly available via the single information point in Article 8. B contradicts this public-availability requirement by proposing confidential, case-by-case negotiation. C invents a Commission pre-approval step not found in the text. D reverses the timing: conditions must be made available in advance through the single information point, not published reactively after a request arrives.

items.stem: A public sector body grants one applicant more favourable access terms than other applicants requesting re-use of the same statistically confidential dataset, in order to favour a domestic company. Is this condition compliant with Article 5(2)?

options.text: No, because conditions for re-use must be non-discriminatory, transparent, proportionate and objectively justified, and must not be used to restrict competition.
Yes, because Article 5(2) allows Member States to set preferential re-use terms for domestic re-users.
Yes, provided the favoured company later shares its analytical results with the public sector body.
No, but only because the dataset in this scenario is statistically confidential; the same condition would be permitted for other categories of data.

items.rationale: Article 5(2) requires conditions for re-use to be non-discriminatory, transparent, proportionate and objectively justified, and states they shall not be used to restrict competition — favouring one applicant over others on nationality grounds violates this. B invents a domestic-preference carve-out that does not exist in the text. C invents an irrelevant condition (sharing results) that has no bearing on the discrimination violation. D wrongly narrows the rule to statistically confidential data only; Article 5(2)'s non-discrimination requirement applies to conditions for re-use generally, across all categories in Article 3(1).

items.stem: Under Article 5(3)(c), when may a public sector body limit re-use to access within the physical premises housing its secure processing environment, rather than allowing remote access?

options.text: Where remote access cannot be allowed without jeopardising the rights and interests of third parties, and access is provided in accordance with high security standards.
Whenever a re-user requests on-site access purely for their own convenience.
Only where the data is protected exclusively on grounds of statistical confidentiality.
Whenever the volume of data requested exceeds a threshold set by the public sector body.

items.rationale: Article 5(3)(c) permits requiring on-premises access, in accordance with high security standards, only where remote access cannot be allowed without jeopardising the rights and interests of third parties. B substitutes re-user convenience for the actual legal trigger (risk to third-party rights). C wrongly restricts the provision to one Article 3(1) category, when it applies generally to protected data. D invents a data-volume threshold that appears nowhere in the text.

items.stem: A re-user is granted re-use of anonymised personal data under Article 5(3), subject to the confidentiality obligation described in Article 5(5). Under that provision, what is the re-user required and prohibited to do with respect to the data subjects concerned?

options.text: The re-user is prohibited from re-identifying any data subject, must take technical and operational measures to prevent re-identification, and must notify the public sector body of any data breach resulting in re-identification.
The re-user must destroy the data immediately after analysis, but re-identification of data subjects is not itself restricted.
The re-user may re-identify data subjects if it separately obtains the public sector body's consent to do so.
The re-user must notify the data subjects directly of any re-identification breach, and has no corresponding duty to notify the public sector body.

items.rationale: Article 5(5) prohibits re-users from re-identifying any data subject, requires them to take technical and operational measures to prevent re-identification, and requires notifying the public sector body of any data breach resulting in re-identification. B contradicts the explicit re-identification prohibition by omitting it. C invents a consent-based exception that the text does not provide. D reverses the notification duty: the obligation runs to the public sector body, not directly to the data subjects, for a re-identification breach.

items.stem: A company operates a platform that lets individuals make their personal data available to potential data users and, in particular, exercise their data portability rights under Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Under Article 10 of the Data Governance Act, which category of data intermediation service does this platform provide?

options.text: Intermediation services between data subjects seeking to make personal data available and potential data users, in particular enabling the exercise of data subjects' rights under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Intermediation services between data holders and potential data users, including bilateral or multilateral exchange platforms.
Services of data cooperatives.
A service exempt from Article 10 because it facilitates a legal right rather than a commercial exchange.

items.rationale: Article 10(b) covers intermediation services between data subjects (or natural persons making non-personal data available) and potential data users, and specifically calls out enabling exercise of data subjects' rights under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 — exactly this platform. B describes the separate 10(a) category, aimed at data holders generally rather than data subjects exercising personal rights. C describes the distinct 10(c) category of data cooperatives. D invents an exemption; all three listed categories, including 10(b), are subject to Article 10's requirements.

items.stem: Article 10 lists three categories of data intermediation services: intermediation between data holders and data users, intermediation between data subjects or natural persons and data users, and services of data cooperatives. What common regulatory requirement applies to the provision of all three categories?

options.text: Provision of the services shall comply with Article 12 and shall be subject to a notification procedure.
Provision of the services requires prior authorisation from the European Commission before operations may begin.
Provision of the services is restricted to public sector bodies.
Provision of the services requires accreditation under Article 3.

items.rationale: Article 10's chapeau states that provision of all three listed data intermediation services shall comply with Article 12 and shall be subject to a notification procedure. B overstates the requirement as prior Commission authorisation rather than notification. C is a misconception: data intermediation services under Article 10 are typically provided by private-sector entities, not restricted to public sector bodies. D conflates this with Article 3, which concerns categories of protected public sector data, not intermediary accreditation.

items.stem: A Member State establishes a national policy under Article 16 to assist data subjects in voluntarily making personal data held by public sector bodies available for data altruism. What must that Member State do as a result of developing this national policy?

options.text: Notify the Commission of the national policy.
Obtain prior approval of the policy from the European Data Innovation Board before implementing it.
Make the policy binding on all other Member States as a harmonised framework.
Register the policy through the single information point referred to in Article 8.

items.rationale: Article 16 states that if a Member State develops such national policies for data altruism, it shall notify the Commission thereof. B invents a prior-approval requirement that Article 16 does not impose. C overstates the effect of a national policy, which remains national under Article 16 with no EU-wide binding force. D conflates Article 16's Commission-notification duty with the distinct single-information-point mechanism used for re-use conditions under Articles 5 and 8.

items.stem: A national mapping agency's public task, as defined by national law, covers producing topographic maps for government use. The agency separately holds internal drafts created for a side project that falls outside that defined public task. Under the Open Data Directive, how does the Directive treat re-use of those side-project documents?

options.text: They fall outside the scope of the Directive, since it does not apply to documents whose supply is an activity outside the public task as defined by law or binding rules
They are covered by the Directive, but the agency may charge full cost recovery for them
They must be released free of charge because they are not listed as high-value datasets
They fall within scope, and re-use may be refused only where a third party holds intellectual property rights over them

items.rationale: Article 1(2)(a) excludes from the Directive's scope documents whose supply falls outside the public task of the body concerned, as defined by law or common administrative practice. (B) wrongly imports the Article 6 charging regime, which presupposes in-scope documents. (C) is irrelevant: high-value dataset status (Articles 13-14) governs charging/format for in-scope datasets, not basic scope. (D) confuses this exclusion with the separate scope exclusion in Article 1(2)(c) for third-party IP rights.

items.stem: Under a Member State's access regime, a document contains personal data whose access is restricted on data-protection grounds, and national law has defined re-use of that data as incompatible with the rules on protection of individuals with regard to processing of personal data. What is the effect on the Directive's application to that document?

options.text: The document, or the personal-data part concerned, falls outside the scope of the Directive under its own exclusion for such data
The document stays within scope, and it is only the Directive's general clause that it is without prejudice to Union data-protection law that removes the obligation to allow re-use
The document falls outside scope only if it also concerns national security
The document stays within scope but must be anonymised before any re-use may be granted

items.rationale: Article 1(2)(h) directly excludes from scope documents (or parts of documents) access to which is restricted on personal-data-protection grounds where re-use has been defined by law as incompatible with data-protection rules. (B) is the trap: Article 1(4) is a general non-prejudice/saving clause about data-protection law generally, not the operative scope exclusion, so it is not what does the excluding here. (C) wrongly imports the national-security exclusion ground from Article 1(2)(d)(i) as an added condition. (D) invents an anonymisation route not stated by the article, which simply removes such documents from scope rather than conditioning release on anonymisation.

items.stem: A citizen asks a public sector body to re-use information that does not exist as a discrete document; producing it would require creating a new document involving more than a simple operation. Does Article 5 require the body to create this document to satisfy the request?

options.text: No, Article 5 does not oblige public sector bodies to create or adapt documents, or provide extracts, where this would involve disproportionate effort beyond a simple operation
Yes, provided the requester pays the marginal cost of reproduction and dissemination
Yes, but only where the requested information belongs to a dataset listed as high-value under Article 14
No, and the body must also cease all future production of that type of document

items.rationale: Article 5(3) states that the format/availability obligation in paragraph 1 does not imply a duty to create or adapt documents or provide extracts where this would involve disproportionate effort beyond a simple operation. (B) wrongly substitutes the Article 6 charging mechanism for the separate creation-obligation question in Article 5(3). (C) misapplies Article 14, which concerns format, API and charging for datasets that already exist, not a duty to create non-existent ones. (D) reverses Article 5(4), which says bodies are not required to continue production for re-use purposes — it does not require them to stop producing the document.

items.stem: A public sector body operates sensors generating dynamic data. Making that data available for re-use immediately after collection via a suitable API would exceed the body's financial and technical capacity. What does Article 5 permit in this situation?

options.text: The data may instead be made available within a time frame, or subject to temporary technical restrictions, that do not unduly impair exploitation of its economic and social potential
The body is permanently exempted from ever making that dynamic data available for re-use
The body may make the data available only once the requester covers the full cost of building the API
The dataset must automatically be added to the list of high-value datasets to qualify for the delay

items.rationale: Article 5(6) allows dynamic data to be made available within a time frame or with temporary technical restrictions, rather than immediately, when immediate provision would impose disproportionate effort exceeding the body's financial and technical capacity — it does not create a full exemption. (B) overstates this as a permanent opt-out. (C) wrongly imports full-cost charging, whereas Article 6 caps recoverable charges at marginal cost. (D) confuses this capacity-based timing exception with the separate high-value dataset listing mechanism under Article 14, which is not a precondition for it.

items.stem: Under Article 6, what is the default rule on charging for re-use of documents, and what limited cost recovery does it permit?

options.text: Re-use is free of charge, but recovery of the marginal costs of reproduction, provision and dissemination, plus costs of anonymising personal data and protecting commercially confidential information, may be allowed
Re-use is free of charge with no exceptions, and no costs of any kind may ever be recovered
Re-use is charged at full cost recovery by default, unless the body opts into free re-use
Re-use is free of charge, and only reproduction costs may be recovered, excluding provision, dissemination or anonymisation costs

items.rationale: Article 6(1) sets the default that re-use is free of charge, while allowing recovery of marginal costs for reproduction, provision and dissemination, and for anonymisation of personal data and protection of commercially confidential information. (B) ignores this permitted marginal-cost recovery. (C) reverses the stated default. (D) understates the recoverable cost categories the article actually lists.

items.stem: Article 6(2) lists categories of bodies to which the general free-of-charge rule for re-use does not apply, by way of exception. Which of the following is one of those listed categories?

options.text: Public undertakings
Research funding organisations
Public service broadcasters
Educational establishments of secondary level and below

items.rationale: Article 6(2)(c) names public undertakings, alongside bodies required to generate revenue to cover a substantial part of their costs (point a) and libraries, university libraries, museums and archives (point b), as exceptions to the free-of-charge default. (B) research funding organisations are not on this charging-exception list; they instead appear in the Article 1(2)(l) scope exclusion for non-research documents. (C) public service broadcasters are excluded from the Directive's scope entirely under Article 1(2)(i), a different effect from merely being exempted from free charging. (D) secondary and lower educational establishments are likewise excluded from scope under Article 1(2)(k), not listed as a charging exception under Article 6(2).

items.stem: A high-value dataset within a thematic category listed in Annex I is held by a public undertaking. Making it available free of charge, as generally required for high-value datasets, would cause a distortion of competition in the relevant market. What does the Directive provide for this situation?

options.text: The implementing acts adopted under Article 14(1) must provide, by way of derogation, that the free-of-charge requirement does not apply to that dataset
The dataset remains free of charge regardless, because high-value datasets are made free of charge for the user without exception
The public undertaking may charge, but only after obtaining a Member State exemption lasting no more than two years, as applies to revenue-generating public sector bodies
The dataset is excluded from the Directive's scope altogether because the public undertaking's activity is directly exposed to competition

items.rationale: Article 14(3) requires that, by derogation from the free-of-charge requirement in Article 14(1), the implementing acts provide that free availability does not apply to high-value datasets held by public undertakings where it would distort competition. (B) ignores that the free-of-charge rule for high-value datasets in Article 6(6)(a) is itself expressly made subject to Article 14(3)-(5). (C) wrongly transplants the two-year budget-impact exemption in Article 14(5), which applies to revenue-dependent public sector bodies, onto the competition-distortion derogation for public undertakings in Article 14(3), which carries no such time limit. (D) conflates the Article 1(2)(b)(ii) scope exclusion for public-undertaking documents from activities directly exposed to competition (a different provision, concerning documents not covered by the Directive at all) with the Article 14(3) charging derogation, which applies to datasets already within scope.

items.stem: Which instrument does the Commission use to adopt the actual list of specific high-value datasets referred to in Article 14(1), as distinct from the thematic categories of high-value datasets set out in Annex I?

options.text: Implementing acts, adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 16(2)
Delegated acts, adopted in accordance with Article 15
A non-binding recommendation addressed to the Member States
A regulation adopted by the ordinary legislative procedure with the European Parliament

items.rationale: Article 14(1) provides that the Commission adopts implementing acts, following the examination procedure in Article 16(2), to lay down the list of specific high-value datasets. (B) is the trap: delegated acts under Article 15 are the instrument used under Article 13(2) to amend the thematic categories in Annex I, a different act with a different procedure, not to list specific datasets. (C) understates the binding implementing-act mechanism actually specified. (D) misattributes the process to ordinary legislative procedure, which plays no role in Article 14(1).

items.stem: A national tax authority (a public authority) intends to put into service a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III, other than a system falling under point 2 of that Annex, to flag tax returns for audit. Under Article 49(3), what must the tax authority do before putting the system into service?

options.text: Register itself, select the system, and register its use in the EU database referred to in Article 71.
Confirm that the provider has already registered the system and take no further action.
Register the system only if it originates from a provider established outside the Union.
Notify the national data protection authority instead of using the EU database.

items.rationale: Article 49(3): deployers that are public authorities (or Union institutions/bodies/offices/agencies) shall register themselves, select the system, and register its use in the EU database referred to in Article 71, before putting into service a high-risk system listed in Annex III (except point 2). B conflates this with the provider's separate registration duty under Art. 49(1); the two obligations run in parallel, not as substitutes for one another. C invents a geographic limitation absent from the text — the deployer duty is not conditioned on the provider's establishment. D swaps the correct recipient (EU database under Art. 71) for a sibling authority (the DPA), which plays no role in Art. 49 registration.

items.stem: A regional public employment office plans to deploy a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III (not one of the systems in point 2 of that Annex) to help decide which jobseekers receive a training subsidy. Before putting the system into service, what must the office do because it is a body governed by public law?

options.text: Perform an assessment of the impact on fundamental rights that the system's use may produce.
Rely on the provider's conformity assessment as sufficient evidence of compliance.
Submit a data protection impact assessment in place of any fundamental rights review.
Wait for the AI Office to issue prior authorisation for the deployment.

items.rationale: Article 27(1) requires deployers that are bodies governed by public law (among others) to perform a fundamental rights impact assessment (FRIA) prior to deploying such high-risk systems, except point 2 of Annex III. B wrongly substitutes the provider's conformity-assessment obligation (a separate, provider-side duty) for the deployer's own FRIA duty. C misreads Art. 27(4): a DPIA does not replace the FRIA — where obligations overlap, the FRIA must complement the DPIA, not stand in for it via submission of the DPIA alone. D invents an authorisation step; Art. 27(5) only tasks the AI Office with developing a questionnaire template, not approving deployments.

items.stem: A ministry, acting as a public-authority deployer, checks the EU database referred to in Article 71 before putting into service a high-risk AI system and discovers that the system it planned to use has not been registered there. According to Article 26(8), what must the ministry do?

options.text: Refrain from using the system and inform the provider or the distributor.
Use the system provisionally while registration is completed retroactively.
Register the system itself in place of the provider so that deployment can proceed.
Notify only the market surveillance authority and proceed with deployment.

items.rationale: Article 26(8): when such deployers find that the envisaged high-risk system has not been registered in the EU database, they 'shall not use that system and shall inform the provider or the distributor.' B is a converse error — no provisional-use-pending-retroactive-registration mechanism exists in the text; the obligation is to stop, not proceed. C misassigns the registration duty: registering the system itself (as opposed to registering the deployer's own use under Art. 49(3)) belongs to the provider or authorised representative under Art. 49(1), not to the deployer as a workaround. D both omits the required notice to the provider/distributor and wrongly permits deployment to continue, contradicting the explicit prohibition on use.

items.stem: A public-authority deployer uses a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III to assist in deciding whether an individual qualifies for a public benefit. Under Article 26(11), what must the deployer do regarding that individual?

options.text: Inform the natural person that they are subject to the use of the high-risk AI system.
Obtain the individual's prior informed consent before the system may be used in their case.
Disclose the underlying algorithm's source code to the individual upon request.
Provide the information only if the individual first files a formal complaint.

items.rationale: Article 26(11): deployers of Annex III high-risk systems that make or assist decisions related to natural persons 'shall inform the natural persons that they are subject to the use of the high-risk AI system.' B overstates the obligation as a consent requirement, which the text does not impose — only information is required. C overstates scope by inventing a source-code disclosure duty not present in Art. 26(11). D is a converse error: the duty to inform is proactive and unconditional ('shall inform'), not triggered only by the individual's own complaint.

items.stem: A public university performed a fundamental rights impact assessment before first using a high-risk AI system to rank scholarship applicants. It now plans to use a similar high-risk AI system for a comparable scholarship-ranking process in a different faculty. Under Article 27(2), must the university carry out an entirely new fundamental rights impact assessment for this second deployment?

options.text: No, the deployer may rely on the previously conducted fundamental rights impact assessment in similar cases.
Yes, a completely new assessment is mandatory for every separate deployment of a high-risk system.
No, once performed the assessment never needs to be revisited even if the process later changes materially.
Yes, but only the provider, not the deployer, may decide whether to reuse the earlier assessment.

items.rationale: Article 27(2): the FRIA obligation applies to the first use, and the deployer 'may, in similar cases, rely on previously conducted fundamental rights impact assessments.' B overstates the rule, ignoring the explicit reuse allowance for similar cases. C is a converse error — Art. 27(2) also requires the deployer to update the assessment if any element 'has changed or is no longer up to date,' so it is not permanently frozen. D misattributes the reuse decision, which the text assigns to the deployer, not the provider.

items.stem: After completing a fundamental rights impact assessment under Article 27(1), a public-authority deployer must notify a specific authority of the results under Article 27(3). Which authority must be notified, and using what document?

options.text: The market surveillance authority, using the filled-out template referred to in Article 27(5).
The AI Office, using a self-drafted summary report with no prescribed template.
The national data protection authority, using the data protection impact assessment template.
The provider of the high-risk AI system, using the instructions for use as the notification document.

items.rationale: Article 27(3): the deployer notifies the market surveillance authority of the assessment's results, submitting the filled-out template referred to in paragraph 5 as part of the notification. B misassigns the recipient — the AI Office's only role under Art. 27(5) is to develop that template, not to receive notifications. C conflates the FRIA notification with the separate GDPR data protection impact assessment process; the named recipient in Art. 27(3) is the market surveillance authority, not the DPA. D confuses this deployer-to-authority reporting duty with unrelated deployer-provider information flows found elsewhere in the Regulation (e.g., Art. 26(5)); the provider is not the Art. 27(3) notification recipient.

items.stem: A national migration authority puts into service a high-risk AI system listed in point 6 of Annex III (migration, asylum and border control management). Under Article 49(4), how must this system's registration be recorded in the EU database?

options.text: In a secure non-public section, containing only the specified information items and accessible solely to the Commission and the national authorities referred to in Article 74(8).
In the same publicly accessible section used for all other high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III.
It is exempt from registration entirely because of the sensitivity of migration-related processing.
In a section accessible to any market surveillance authority in any Member State without restriction.

items.rationale: Article 49(4): for systems under points 1, 6 and 7 of Annex III (law enforcement, migration, asylum, border control management), registration is made in a secure non-public section of the EU database, limited to the listed information items, and access is restricted to the Commission and the national authorities referred to in Article 74(8). B overstates uniformity by ignoring the explicit non-public carve-out for these sensitive categories. C overstates the exception — the system must still be registered, only the access channel is restricted, not the obligation itself. D overstates access, granting it to any Member State market surveillance authority rather than the specifically named Commission and Art. 74(8) authorities.

items.stem: A private company operating critical infrastructure deploys a high-risk AI system falling under point 2 of Annex III (safety components in the management of critical infrastructure). Under Article 27(1), is this deployer required to carry out a fundamental rights impact assessment for that system?

options.text: No, because Article 27(1) expressly excludes high-risk systems intended to be used in the area listed in point 2 of Annex III from that obligation.
Yes, because all deployers of Annex III high-risk systems must perform a fundamental rights impact assessment without exception.
No, because private companies are entirely outside the personal scope of Article 27, regardless of the system's Annex III category.
Yes, but only after the system has caused a serious incident under Article 73.

items.rationale: Article 27(1) opens with an explicit carve-out: the FRIA duty applies to deployers listed there 'with the exception of high-risk AI systems intended to be used in the area listed in point 2 of Annex III.' B overstates scope, ignoring this explicit exception. C is the wrong reasoning for a right-shaped answer: Art. 27(1) does reach private entities in some circumstances (e.g., private entities providing public services, or systems under points 5(b)/(c) of Annex III), so the exemption here turns on the point 2 subject matter, not on being a private company. D invents an incident-triggered timing; Art. 27(1) makes the FRIA a 'prior to deploying' obligation, not one contingent on a later serious incident.

items.stem: According to the European Commission's High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence (HLEG), 'Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI' (2019), trustworthy AI throughout its life cycle should be built on three components. Which set correctly identifies them?

options.text: Lawful, ethical, and robust
Transparent, fair, and accountable
Safe, secure, and privacy-preserving
Human-centric, sustainable, and innovative

items.rationale: The HLEG guidelines state trustworthy AI must be lawful (complying with laws and regulations), ethical (respecting ethical principles and values), and robust (both technically and socially, since even good-intentioned AI can cause unintentional harm). B lists three of the seven downstream key requirements, not the top-level components — a category-level confusion. C narrows 'robust' to only its cybersecurity/safety dimension, dropping lawful and ethical entirely. D substitutes generic policy buzzwords that do not appear as the HLEG's structural triad.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: A compliance team is structuring an internal AI audit checklist directly on the framework set out in the HLEG Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (2019). How many key requirements does that document specify for achieving trustworthy AI in practice?

options.text: Five
Six
Seven
Eight

items.rationale: The HLEG guidelines specify seven key requirements: human agency and oversight; technical robustness and safety; privacy and data governance; transparency; diversity, non-discrimination and fairness; societal and environmental wellbeing; and accountability. Five is a plausible trap for those recalling the OECD AI Principles (2019), which set out five values-based principles — a sibling-document confusion. Six under-counts by merging two adjacent requirements. Eight over-counts, often from mistakenly splitting 'diversity, non-discrimination and fairness' into two separate items.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: The HLEG subsequently released the Assessment List for Trustworthy AI (ALTAI) as a companion tool to the 2019 Ethics Guidelines. What is ALTAI's primary function?

options.text: A self-assessment checklist that helps developers and deployers operationalize the seven key requirements
A binding conformity assessment procedure required before CE marking under the EU AI Act
A statistical benchmark suite for measuring model accuracy across NLP tasks
A certification scheme administered by EU national market surveillance authorities

items.rationale: ALTAI is a practical, voluntary self-assessment tool that translates the seven key requirements into concrete questions for organizations to apply to their own systems. B wrongly imports the EU AI Act's separate, legally binding conformity-assessment/CE-marking regime — a sibling-regulation trap, since ALTAI itself creates no legal obligation. C confuses an ethics/governance tool with a technical accuracy benchmark. D overstates ALTAI's status as formal certification, when no such statutory certification body or scheme was created by the HLEG.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, The Assessment List for Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence (ALTAI), European Commission (2020)

items.stem: A hospital deploys a clinical-decision-support AI that a physician can override at any point, and whose interface always signals that it is presenting a recommendation rather than a final decision. This design most directly implements which HLEG key requirement?

options.text: Human agency and oversight
Technical robustness and safety
Transparency
Accountability

items.rationale: Human agency and oversight is the requirement addressing human-in-the-loop control and the ability to override or intervene in an AI system's decisions. C is a tempting distractor because signaling that output is a recommendation looks informational, but transparency in the HLEG sense concerns traceability, explainability, and honest communication about system capabilities, not override capability itself. B is a trap conflating override safeguards with fail-safe/technical-fault resilience. D wrongly attributes the design feature to auditability and redress mechanisms rather than to the oversight requirement it actually instantiates.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: An HR-tech vendor argues that because its hiring-recommendation model was trained on a dataset reflecting the company's actual historical hiring outcomes, any resulting adverse effect on a protected group cannot violate the HLEG 'diversity, non-discrimination and fairness' requirement, since the model is merely reproducing real-world data. Why is this argument inconsistent with the guidelines?

options.text: The guidelines require identifying and mitigating unfair bias even when it originates in historical training data, not only bias introduced during model design
The guidelines only prohibit intentional discrimination, so unintentional statistical bias inherited from historical data is out of scope
The guidelines defer entirely to the definition of discrimination in the EU's Race Equality Directive, which does not address algorithmic bias
The requirement applies only at the deployment stage, so bias present in the training data falls outside its scope

items.rationale: The HLEG requirement explicitly calls for avoiding unfair bias across the whole AI life cycle, including data collection and preparation, since historical datasets routinely encode existing societal discrimination. B is a converse-style trap treating only intent-based discrimination as relevant, contrary to the guidelines' focus on outcomes and unfair bias regardless of intent. C wrongly subordinates the ethics guidelines to a separate legal instrument that was not designed to address algorithmic bias — a sibling-regulation trap. D overstates the requirement's scope as deployment-only, when data governance and training-stage bias are explicitly in scope.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: A team preparing an ALTAI self-assessment for a facial-recognition product treats the HLEG requirement 'privacy and data governance' as fully satisfied simply because the product has completed a GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA). Which of the following best describes the flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: The HLEG requirement extends beyond legal compliance with data protection law to also address data quality, integrity, and access protocols across the AI system's full life cycle
GDPR does not apply to biometric data, so the completed DPIA is legally irrelevant to the assessment
ALTAI requirements apply only to AI systems developed outside the European Economic Area
The privacy and data governance requirement was removed from the final 2019 guidelines and replaced by a reference to the ePrivacy Directive

items.rationale: The HLEG's privacy and data governance requirement is broader than statutory data protection compliance: it also covers data quality and integrity, relevance of the data gathered, and access protocols throughout the system's life cycle, so a DPIA alone does not exhaust it. B is factually wrong — biometric data is 'special category' data squarely covered by GDPR, making this an incorrect-fact trap. C invents a jurisdictional carve-out that does not exist; ALTAI is meant for use by any developer or deployer regardless of location. D fabricates a document-history fact — the requirement was not removed, and the guidelines do not reference the ePrivacy Directive as a replacement.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: A public-sector AI system fully complies with all applicable EU and national statutes, but its automated scoring disproportionately disadvantages a protected group in a way participants find morally objectionable, even though the outcome is not currently unlawful. Under the HLEG framework's three-component structure, how should this case be characterized?

options.text: It satisfies the lawful component but fails the ethical component
It satisfies the ethical component but fails the lawful component
It fails both the lawful and the robust components
It satisfies all three components, because no statute was violated

items.rationale: The HLEG explicitly separates lawfulness from ethics: a system can comply fully with existing law while still failing to respect ethical principles and values, which is precisely the scenario described. B commits a converse error, reversing which component is satisfied and which fails. C is factually wrong on lawfulness (the system is compliant) and additionally misapplies 'robust,' which concerns technical/social resilience, not moral acceptability. D collapses lawful compliance into full trustworthiness, ignoring that the ethical component is a distinct, non-reducible requirement in the framework.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: Under the HLEG key requirement 'societal and environmental wellbeing,' assessment of an AI system's impact is understood to extend to which of the following considerations?

options.text: Effects on democratic processes and the broader social fabric, in addition to environmental sustainability
Only the carbon footprint of training compute, since environmental wellbeing is defined narrowly as energy consumption
Only effects on individual end users, since societal-level impacts fall under the accountability requirement
Only compliance with the EU Taxonomy Regulation's environmental sustainability criteria

items.rationale: The HLEG guidelines describe societal and environmental wellbeing broadly, covering impacts on society at large and democratic processes as well as environmental sustainability, not a single narrow metric. B improperly narrows environmental wellbeing to compute carbon footprint alone, ignoring broader ecological and resource impacts the guidelines describe. C incorrectly relocates societal-level effects to the accountability requirement, which instead concerns auditability, redress, and responsibility allocation. D imports an unrelated EU financial-sustainability regulation (the Taxonomy Regulation) as if it defined the HLEG requirement — a sibling-regulation trap, since the two instruments are independent and were not linked by the HLEG.

source.title: High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, European Commission (2019)

items.stem: The Commission proposes, and the co-legislators adopt, an EU act that is described in its own text as binding in its entirety and directly applicable in every Member State, requiring no national transposition measure. Under Article 288 TFEU, which category of legal instrument has been adopted?

options.text: A regulation
A directive
A decision addressed to all Member States
A recommendation

items.rationale: Article 288(2) TFEU: a regulation has general application, is binding in its entirety, and is directly applicable in all Member States without transposition. (B) A directive is binding only as to the result to be achieved and leaves national authorities the choice of form and methods, requiring transposition — the opposite of 'no national transposition measure' (Art 288(3)). (C) A decision is binding in its entirety only on those to whom it is addressed; even one addressed to all Member States is not 'directly applicable' in the generalised, self-executing sense reserved to regulations, and decisions need not have general application. (D) Recommendations and opinions have no binding force at all (Art 288(5)).

source.title: Consolidated Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Article 288

items.stem: Under the ordinary legislative procedure, the European Parliament and the Council fail to approve a joint text at second reading. Per Article 294 TFEU, what happens next?

options.text: A Conciliation Committee, composed of equal numbers of Council and Parliament representatives, is convened to reach agreement on a joint text within six weeks
The European Council convenes an extraordinary summit to arbitrate the disagreement between Parliament and Council
The Commission conducts a further reading of its own to amend the proposal in light of both institutions' positions
The matter is referred to the Court of Justice for a binding ruling settling the disputed text

items.rationale: Article 294(10)-(11) TFEU: absent agreement at second reading, a Conciliation Committee with equal numbers of Council and Parliament members (the Commission participates to reconcile positions) is convened and has six weeks to approve a joint text. (B) The European Council (Art 15 TEU) has no formal role in resolving OLP disagreements — this conflates it with the Council, a common terminology trap. (C) The Commission has no formal 'reading' stage in the procedure; its role is proposal, opinion-giving and possible withdrawal, not amendment at this stage. (D) The CJEU adjudicates legality, not legislative content disputes between co-legislators.

source.title: Consolidated Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Article 294

items.stem: The co-legislators confer on the Commission a power to specify uniform technical conditions under which national customs authorities are to apply an already-adopted regulation, without altering or supplementing any element of that regulation. Under the Treaty framework governing conferred implementing power, this power should be conferred as:

options.text: An implementing act under Article 291(2) TFEU
A delegated act under Article 290 TFEU
A measure under the regulatory procedure with scrutiny
A decision adopted under Article 288 TFEU on the Commission's own initiative

items.rationale: Article 291(2) TFEU: where uniform conditions are needed for implementing a legally binding Union act that does not itself require amendment or supplementation, the conferred power is an implementing act. (B) Delegated acts under Art 290 exist specifically to amend or supplement non-essential elements of the legislative act — the scenario expressly excludes this, so classifying it as delegated confuses the two Lisbon-era categories. (C) The regulatory procedure with scrutiny was the pre-Lisbon comitology mechanism (1999/2006 Comitology Decision) and has no legal basis under the current Art 290/291 framework — citing it is an obsolete-instrument trap. (D) The Commission cannot self-confer implementing or delegated power; it can only exercise power expressly conferred by the legislative act, so 'own initiative' overstates its autonomy.

source.title: Consolidated Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Articles 290-291

items.stem: Under Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 laying down rules on Member State control of Commission implementing powers, a committee of Member State representatives delivers a negative opinion on a draft implementing act by qualified majority. Under which procedure does this negative opinion prevent the Commission from adopting the draft act (subject only to a possible referral to an appeal committee)?

options.text: The examination procedure
The advisory procedure
The regulatory procedure with scrutiny
The urgency procedure under Article 8 of the Regulation

items.rationale: Article 5 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011: under the examination procedure (used for implementing acts of general scope and in sensitive areas such as agriculture, taxation, trade and environment), a negative opinion adopted by qualified majority means the Commission shall not adopt the draft act, though it may refer the matter to an appeal committee. (B) Under the advisory procedure (Art 4), the Commission takes 'utmost account' of the committee's opinion but is not bound by it, so a negative opinion does not block adoption — the converse of what the stem describes. (C) The regulatory procedure with scrutiny was abolished with the entry into force of the current comitology regime and does not exist under Regulation 182/2011. (D) Article 8's urgency procedure allows immediate application of an act pending the committee's opinion; it addresses timing, not the blocking effect of a negative opinion.

source.title: Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 February 2011, Article 5

items.stem: In Case C-270/12 (United Kingdom v Parliament and Council, the 'ESMA short-selling' case), the Court of Justice upheld conferring on a Union agency the power to adopt emergency measures restricting short selling, distinguishing this delegation from the discretionary-powers delegation condemned in Case 9/56 (Meroni v High Authority). What was the key distinguishing factor the Court relied on?

options.text: The agency's power was circumscribed by precisely defined conditions and criteria limiting its discretion, and was subject to procedural safeguards and judicial review, unlike the open-ended discretion at issue in Meroni
The agency had been established by a regulation of the co-legislators rather than by a Commission decision, and that legal form alone justified broader delegation
Because ESMA is a Union body rather than a private entity, the Meroni doctrine on delegation of discretionary powers does not apply to it at all
The Court held that Meroni had been implicitly overruled and no longer constrains the delegation of discretionary powers to any Union body

items.rationale: The Court held ESMA's power was conferred subject to conditions and criteria that limit its discretion, coupled with procedural safeguards and reasoned decisions reviewable by the EU courts — satisfying, rather than escaping, the Meroni requirements for delegation of powers involving a margin of discretion. (B) The legal instrument establishing the agency (regulation vs. decision) is not the criterion the Court used to distinguish the cases. (C) The Court explicitly confirmed Meroni applies to delegations to Union bodies and agencies, not only to private entities — the opposite of what this option claims. (D) Meroni was applied and distinguished, not overruled; it remains the governing framework for assessing delegation of discretionary power to EU bodies.

source.title: Judgment of the Court (Grand Chamber), Case C-270/12, United Kingdom v European Parliament and Council of the European Union, ECLI:EU:C:2014:18

items.stem: A national court hearing a pending civil case is uncertain whether a directly effective EU regulation should be interpreted in a way that conflicts with the applicable national provision, and no separate national procedure exists to test the regulation's validity. Which procedure allows the national court to obtain a binding ruling from the Court of Justice while the national proceedings remain pending?

options.text: The preliminary ruling procedure under Article 267 TFEU
Infringement proceedings under Article 258 TFEU brought by the Commission
An action for annulment under Article 263 TFEU brought by the national court
An action for failure to act under Article 265 TFEU

items.rationale: Article 267 TFEU allows any national court, in the course of pending proceedings, to refer questions on the interpretation or validity of EU law to the CJEU, with the national case remaining stayed pending the ruling. (B) Article 258 infringement actions run between the Commission and a Member State; a national court hearing a private dispute has no role in triggering or benefiting from that separate procedure. (C) Standing to bring an annulment action under Article 263 is reserved to privileged/semi-privileged applicants, Member States, institutions, or individuals directly and individually concerned — a national court cannot bring such an action, and it is a free-standing action, not incidental to pending national litigation. (D) Article 265 addresses institutional failure to act, an unrelated scenario to interpreting EU law in a pending national case.

source.title: Consolidated Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Article 267

items.stem: In Case C-427/12 (Commission v European Parliament and Council, the 'Biocidal Products' case), the CJEU reviewed the EU legislature's choice to require Commission measures setting fee levels payable to the European Chemicals Agency to be adopted as implementing acts rather than delegated acts. What did the Court hold about the legislature's discretion in making that choice?

options.text: The EU legislature enjoys discretion, subject to judicial review for manifest error, to choose between a delegated and an implementing act based on the nature and content of the empowerment conferred, not merely on whether the measure involves some element of assessment
Any Commission measure that requires assessment or evaluation must be adopted as a delegated act, because evaluation necessarily amounts to supplementing the legislative act
The Court ruled that setting the Agency's fees inherently amends an essential element of the basic act and therefore the measure could only ever be classified as a delegated act
The Court held the choice between Article 290 and Article 291 TFEU is a purely political question that is non-justiciable, and it declined to review the legislature's choice

items.rationale: The Court confirmed the legislature has a margin of discretion — reviewable only for manifest error of assessment — when deciding whether to confer power as a delegated or an implementing act, based on the nature and content of the power conferred, and upheld classifying the fee-setting measure as implementing despite it involving some assessment. (B) This was in substance the Parliament's losing argument: mere evaluative content does not automatically require delegated-act status. (C) Factually reversed — the Court upheld the implementing-act classification for the fee measure rather than requiring a delegated act. (D) The Court did exercise judicial review of the legislature's choice (for manifest error); it did not decline jurisdiction.

source.title: Judgment of the Court (Grand Chamber), Case C-427/12, Commission v European Parliament and Council of the European Union, ECLI:EU:C:2014:170

items.stem: A private company wants to rely, in litigation against another private party before a national court, on a provision of an EU directive whose transposition deadline has passed although the Member State has not transposed it. Per settled Court of Justice case law on direct effect, what is the correct outcome?

options.text: The provision cannot be invoked horizontally against the private party, though it may be invoked vertically against the State or an emanation of the State
