The provision automatically produces horizontal direct effect against any party once the transposition deadline has passed, exactly as a regulation would
The provision can never be relied upon before a national court under any circumstances, because directives entirely lack direct effect
The provision must first be annulled by the Court of Justice under Article 263 TFEU before any direct effect analysis becomes possible

items.rationale: Following Marshall and Faccini Dori, an unconditional and sufficiently precise directive provision can be relied on vertically against the State or a state emanation after the transposition deadline, but the Court has consistently refused to recognise horizontal direct effect of directives between private parties. (B) This conflates directives with regulations, which under Article 288 TFEU are directly applicable erga omnes; directives never acquire that automatic universal effect. (C) Overstated: directives can produce vertical direct effect once unconditional and sufficiently precise past the deadline — 'never... any circumstances' is incorrect. (D) Annulment under Article 263 TFEU concerns the validity of an EU act before the EU courts and is analytically unrelated to whether a directive provision has direct effect in national litigation.

source.title: Judgment of the Court, Case C-91/92, Paola Faccini Dori v Recreb Srl, ECLI:EU:C:1994:292

items.stem: A team trains a model for a regression task. Both the training-set MSE and a held-out validation-set MSE are high and close in value to each other. Which single modeling change is the most direct way to address this?

options.text: Increase model capacity (e.g., add more parameters or basis functions)
Increase the L2 regularization coefficient
Collect more training examples without changing the model
Apply early stopping earlier in training

items.rationale: High train and high validation error, close together, is the signature of underfitting (high bias), diagnosed via train/test error curves as in ESL Ch.7. Increasing capacity reduces bias. B (more regularization) increases bias further — the wrong direction. C (more data) addresses high variance/overfitting, not high bias — a classic misconception (confusing the variance remedy with the bias problem). D (earlier early stopping) also increases bias, since early stopping is itself a variance-reduction technique.

source.title: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 7 'Model Assessment and Selection'

items.stem: A data scientist has 500 candidate features for a linear regression model and wants the fitted model to automatically set many coefficients exactly to zero for interpretability. Which regularization approach should be used?

options.text: L1 (Lasso) penalty on the coefficients
L2 (Ridge) penalty on the coefficients
Elastic net penalty with the L1 weight set to zero
L2 penalty combined with a larger learning rate

items.rationale: The L1 penalty's constraint region has corners on the coordinate axes, which induces exact-zero coefficients (Tibshirani, 1996). B: L2 shrinks coefficients toward zero but essentially never to exactly zero — a converse-error distractor (dense shrinkage, not sparsity). C: setting the L1 weight to zero in elastic net reduces it to pure ridge, contradicting the stated goal despite superficially naming L1. D: learning rate is an optimization hyperparameter, unrelated to the geometric sparsity mechanism — a category-error distractor.

source.title: Tibshirani, R. (1996), 'Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso,' Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B

items.stem: During training of a neural network with vanilla stochastic gradient descent, the loss decreases for the first few steps and then begins oscillating with increasing amplitude, eventually diverging to very large values. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The learning rate is too high
The batch size is too large
The loss function is non-convex
The model has too many parameters

items.rationale: An overshoot-then-diverge oscillation pattern is the textbook signature of a step size too large relative to the local curvature (Goodfellow et al., Ch. 8). B: a larger batch size gives a lower-variance gradient estimate and tends to smooth training, not cause divergence — a converse-error distractor. C: non-convexity explains local minima/saddle points, not this specific step-size-driven oscillation signature. D: overparameterization relates to overfitting/generalization, not to optimization divergence.

source.title: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville, Deep Learning, Ch. 8, Sec. 8.3 'Basic Algorithms'

items.stem: An agent controls a simulated robot arm. It receives no labeled examples of correct actions; instead, after each action it receives a scalar reward from the environment and must learn a policy that maximizes cumulative reward over time. Which learning paradigm does this describe?

options.text: Reinforcement learning
Supervised learning
Unsupervised learning
Semi-supervised learning

items.rationale: Sequential decisions plus a scalar reward signal, with no labeled target actions, defines reinforcement learning (Sutton & Barto). B: supervised learning requires labeled input-output pairs, which are absent — the distractor confuses a reward signal with a label. C: unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data without a reward-driven action loop. D: semi-supervised learning mixes labeled and unlabeled data, still not reward-based sequential decision-making.

source.title: Sutton, R.S. & Barto, A.G., Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (2nd ed., 2018), Ch. 1

items.stem: A model outputs a probability distribution over K mutually exclusive classes via a softmax layer. Which loss function is the standard choice for training this model via maximum likelihood?

options.text: Categorical cross-entropy
Mean squared error on the softmax outputs
Hinge loss
Mean absolute error on the softmax outputs

items.rationale: Cross-entropy is the negative log-likelihood under a categorical distribution and pairs naturally with softmax outputs (Goodfellow et al., Ch. 6.2). B: MSE on probabilities mismatches the categorical likelihood and produces weaker gradients under saturation — a common but suboptimal-choice misconception. C: hinge loss is the margin-based loss for classifiers like SVMs, not a probabilistic softmax loss. D: MAE has the same likelihood mismatch as MSE and additionally has a non-smooth gradient at zero.

source.title: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville, Deep Learning, Ch. 6, Sec. 6.2.2 'Output Units'

items.stem: For stochastic gradient descent to be guaranteed to converge to a stationary point under standard stochastic approximation theory, which condition must the sequence of learning rates η_t satisfy?

options.text: Σ η_t = ∞ and Σ η_t² < ∞
η_t must be constant for all t
Σ η_t < ∞ and Σ η_t² = ∞
η_t must increase monotonically with t

items.rationale: The Robbins-Monro conditions require the sum of learning rates to diverge (enough total movement to reach the optimum) while the sum of squares converges (steps shrink fast enough to control gradient noise). B: a constant rate never shrinks, so noise prevents convergence to a point, only bounded oscillation around it. C: reverses which sum must diverge versus converge — a converse-error distractor. D: monotonically increasing rates amplify noise and guarantee divergence rather than convergence.

source.title: Robbins, H. & Monro, S. (1951), 'A Stochastic Approximation Method,' Annals of Mathematical Statistics

items.stem: A team standardizes (zero mean, unit variance) all features using statistics computed from the entire dataset, then splits the data into training and test sets and trains a model. The reported test-set performance turns out to be optimistic compared to true generalization performance. What is the cause?

options.text: Data leakage: test-set information was used to compute the scaling statistics before the split
The model is underfitting due to feature standardization
The test set is too small to give a reliable estimate
Standardization removed information the model needed

items.rationale: Any preprocessing statistic derived using data outside the training fold leaks test information into training, inflating the reported score — the canonical 'wrong way to do cross-validation' error (ESL Sec. 7.10.2). B: underfitting would depress training performance too, not selectively inflate test performance. C: small sample size affects the variance of the estimate, not a systematic optimistic bias. D: standardization is an invertible linear transform and does not remove information.

source.title: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Sec. 7.10.2 'The Wrong and Right Way to Do Cross-Validation'

items.stem: In ridge regression, as the regularization strength λ is increased from zero, what is the expected effect on the bias and variance of the fitted coefficient estimates?

options.text: Bias increases and variance decreases
Bias decreases and variance increases
Both bias and variance decrease
Both bias and variance increase

items.rationale: Shrinking coefficients toward zero biases estimates away from the unconstrained least-squares solution but reduces their sampling variance (ESL Sec. 3.4.1; Bishop Sec. 3.3). B reverses the well-established direction — a converse-error distractor. C ignores that any λ>0 necessarily introduces bias. D contradicts the entire purpose of shrinkage regularization, which is variance reduction.

source.title: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Sec. 3.4.1

items.stem: A researcher has only 200 labeled examples and wants a reliable estimate of a model's generalization performance without sacrificing a large portion of the data to a single held-out set. Which evaluation procedure is most appropriate?

options.text: k-fold cross-validation
A single 90/10 train/test split
Training and testing on the same full dataset
Bootstrapping the test set only, with no separate training data

items.rationale: k-fold cross-validation reuses all data for both training and validation across folds, giving a lower-variance generalization estimate when n is small (Bishop Sec. 1.3; ESL Sec. 7.10). B: a single split on a small sample gives a high-variance estimate that depends heavily on the particular split, which is exactly the problem stated. C: evaluating on training data gives an optimistic, biased estimate — not a validation procedure at all. D: resampling without a genuine held-out training/test partition does not estimate generalization to unseen data.

source.title: Bishop, C., Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning (2006), Sec. 1.3

items.stem: A deep feedforward network with many layers, each using sigmoid activations, trains very slowly, and the gradients in the early layers are observed to be near zero. What is the standard explanation for this phenomenon?

options.text: Repeated multiplication of small sigmoid derivatives during backpropagation shrinks gradients exponentially with depth
The learning rate is too high for a network of this depth
The loss function is not differentiable at the sigmoid's saturation points
The network has too few parameters to represent the target function

items.rationale: Sigmoid derivatives are bounded by 0.25 and saturate near 0/1; chained across many layers via the chain rule this compounds into vanishing gradients in early layers (Glorot & Bengio, 2010; Goodfellow et al., Ch. 8). B: too-high a learning rate produces divergence/oscillation, not systematically near-zero early-layer gradients. C: sigmoid is smooth and differentiable everywhere, including at saturation — the derivative is small, not undefined. D: underparameterization is unrelated to this depth-driven gradient-magnitude phenomenon.

source.title: Glorot, X. & Bengio, Y. (2010), 'Understanding the Difficulty of Training Deep Feedforward Neural Networks,' AISTATS

items.stem: According to the No Free Lunch theorem for optimization and learning, when performance is averaged over all possible problems/data-generating distributions, how do two arbitrary learning algorithms A and B compare?

options.text: Their average performance is identical across all possible problems
Algorithm A with more inductive bias always outperforms B on average
The algorithm using more training data always outperforms the other on average
The algorithm with lower computational complexity always outperforms the other on average

items.rationale: Averaged over all possible data-generating distributions, no algorithm dominates another; real-world superiority comes from matching inductive bias to the actual, non-uniform distribution of problems encountered (Wolpert & Macready, 1997; Goodfellow et al., Sec. 5.4). B overstates scope — more inductive bias helps only on problems matching that bias, which is precisely the theorem's point, not a universal advantage. C: more data helps for a fixed problem but does not break the averaged-over-all-problems equivalence. D: computational efficiency is orthogonal to statistical performance averaged over all problems.

source.title: Wolpert, D.H. & Macready, W.G. (1997), 'No Free Lunch Theorems for Optimization,' IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation

items.stem: A large neural network trained on a limited dataset overfits severely. During training, at each forward pass a random subset of hidden units is temporarily removed along with their connections, and at test time all units are used with their outputs appropriately scaled. Which technique does this describe?

options.text: Dropout
Batch normalization
L2 weight decay
Early stopping

items.rationale: Randomly zeroing units per forward pass during training, with scaling at test time, is the defining mechanism of dropout, which approximates averaging over an ensemble of subnetworks (Srivastava et al., 2014). B: batch normalization normalizes layer activations using batch statistics and does not remove units. C: L2 weight decay penalizes weight magnitude directly in the loss, with no random unit removal. D: early stopping halts training based on validation performance and is unrelated to per-pass unit removal.

source.title: Srivastava, N. et al. (2014), 'Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting,' Journal of Machine Learning Research 15

items.stem: A team preprocessing text for an LLM needs a tokenization method that represents unseen words by splitting them into subword units learned from corpus-frequency statistics, rather than relying on a fixed whole-word vocabulary that fails on out-of-vocabulary terms. Which method, introduced by Sennrich et al. (2016) for neural machine translation and later adopted by GPT-style models, does this describe?

options.text: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)
One-hot word-level tokenization
Fixed-vocabulary whole-word tokenization
Random subword sampling without merge rules

items.rationale: BPE iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent byte/character pairs to build a subword vocabulary, letting rare/unseen words decompose into known pieces. B is wrong because one-hot whole-word encoding is exactly the fixed-vocabulary scheme that causes the out-of-vocabulary problem BPE solves. C restates the rejected fixed-vocabulary approach as if it were the answer. D invents a 'random' variant; BPE's merges are deterministic, driven by frequency statistics, not sampling.

source.title: Sennrich, Haddow & Birch, 'Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units', ACL 2016

items.stem: In the scaled dot-product attention defined by Vaswani et al. (2017), the dot products of queries and keys are divided by the square root of the key dimension d_k before applying softmax. What is the primary reason for this scaling?

options.text: To prevent the dot products from growing large in magnitude and pushing the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients
To normalize the attention weights so they sum exactly to one across all heads
To reduce the number of parameters needed in the query and key projection matrices
To ensure positional encodings remain orthogonal across different sequence positions

items.rationale: The paper notes that for large d_k, unscaled dot products grow large and saturate softmax, giving vanishingly small gradients; dividing by sqrt(d_k) counteracts this. B confuses this with softmax's own normalization property, which holds regardless of scaling and does not operate 'across heads'. C is wrong because scaling is a runtime operation on activations, not a parameter-count reduction technique. D conflates attention scaling with a property of sinusoidal positional encodings, an unrelated mechanism.

source.title: Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need', NeurIPS 2017

items.stem: Vaswani et al. (2017) split attention into multiple parallel heads rather than using one attention function over the full model dimension. What benefit do the authors attribute to this multi-head design?

options.text: It allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions
It reduces the total computational cost of attention compared to single-head attention of equivalent total dimension
It guarantees that each head learns to attend only to adjacent tokens, mimicking a convolution
It eliminates the need for positional encoding because heads implicitly encode position

items.rationale: Multi-head attention lets different heads learn distinct relational patterns across subspaces, which a single averaged attention function would blur. B is false: with head dimension reduced proportionally, total compute is comparable, not reduced; cost efficiency is not the stated motivation. C overstates a possible emergent pattern into a guarantee the paper never makes. D is wrong because positional encodings are still added to embeddings regardless of head count; heads do not substitute for them.

source.title: Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need', NeurIPS 2017

items.stem: A lab trains a series of models following Kaplan et al. (2020)'s original scaling-law recommendations, allocating most additional compute to increasing parameter count while holding training tokens comparatively fixed. Hoffmann et al. (2022, 'Chinchilla') later showed this practice to be compute-suboptimal. According to the Chinchilla analysis, what should have been done instead as compute increases?

options.text: Scale model size and training tokens in roughly equal proportion
Scale training tokens quadratically faster than model size
Hold training tokens fixed and scale only model size, as Kaplan et al. recommended
Scale model size faster than training tokens, since larger models generalize with less data

items.rationale: Chinchilla's fitted scaling law implies model size and data should grow at roughly the same rate for compute-optimal training, which is why Chinchilla (70B params, 1.4T tokens) outperformed the much larger, undertrained Gopher. B invents an incorrect quadratic ratio not supported by the paper. C simply restates the very practice Chinchilla shows to be suboptimal, a converse error. D encodes the opposite misconception, that bigger models need proportionally less data, which the paper directly refutes.

source.title: Hoffmann et al., 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models', 2022 (cf. Kaplan et al., 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models', 2020)

items.stem: Ouyang et al. (2022) describe a three-step procedure for turning GPT-3 into InstructGPT: supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations, training a reward model on human preference comparisons, and then optimizing the policy against that reward model. Which algorithm did they use for the final policy-optimization step?

options.text: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)
Iterative supervised fine-tuning repeated without a reward model
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
Tabular Q-learning over a discrete reward function

items.rationale: InstructGPT's RLHF stage uses PPO to optimize the policy against the learned reward model. B describes only the first step (SFT) and omits the RL stage entirely, a converse/incomplete-process error. C names DPO, a 2023 alternative that reframes preference optimization without an explicit reward model or RL loop, an anachronistic wrong-paper trap. D proposes tabular Q-learning, infeasible for a high-dimensional continuous policy like an LLM and not what was used.

source.title: Ouyang et al., 'Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback', NeurIPS 2022

items.stem: An organization has a base language model trained only with a next-token prediction objective on a large unlabeled corpus, with no instruction tuning or RLHF applied. When prompted with a direct question, this base model will most likely

options.text: continue the text in a plausible way, such as generating a list of similar questions, rather than directly answering as an assistant would
refuse to answer, since safety alignment is inherent to the pretraining objective
answer concisely and helpfully, because next-token prediction alone produces instruction-following behavior
ask a clarifying question first, as instruction-tuned assistants typically do

items.rationale: A raw pretrained model merely continues text distributionally and often mimics the surrounding corpus (e.g., more questions, a forum thread) rather than acting as an assistant. B misattributes refusal behavior, which comes from safety/RLHF fine-tuning, to pretraining itself. C overstates pretraining's capability; instruction-following is a distinct behavior instilled by supervised fine-tuning and RLHF, as InstructGPT-style comparisons demonstrate. D attributes an aligned assistant behavior (seeking clarification) to a model that has never been tuned to do so.

source.title: Ouyang et al., 'Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback', NeurIPS 2022

items.stem: Lewis et al. (2020) introduced retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), combining a pretrained parametric generator with a non-parametric memory accessed via a retriever. Compared to a purely parametric model, what is the primary motivation for this architecture as stated by the authors?

options.text: It allows the model's knowledge to be updated or expanded by revising the retrieval index, without retraining the generator's parameters
It permanently eliminates hallucination, since all outputs are grounded in retrieved text
It removes the need for the generator component to be pretrained on any text corpus
It guarantees lower inference latency than a purely parametric model of equivalent size

items.rationale: RAG's stated advantage is that its non-parametric memory can be directly revised and expanded (e.g., swapping the index) as world knowledge changes, without retraining the generator. B is the classic overstated claim: RAG reduces but does not eliminate hallucination, since generation can still diverge from or misuse retrieved passages. C is false; the generator (e.g., a BART-based seq2seq model) is still pretrained on text, retrieval is additive, not a replacement for pretraining. D is unsupported: adding a retrieval step introduces extra computation and typically increases, not decreases, latency.

source.title: Lewis et al., 'Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks', NeurIPS 2020

items.stem: A team wants to extend a pretrained transformer's effective context window beyond the length seen during training, without retraining from scratch, by modifying how relative position information is computed at inference time. Which technique, exemplified by Position Interpolation for rotary-embedding models, describes this approach?

options.text: Rescaling the position indices fed into rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) so longer sequences map into the range seen during training
Increasing the number of attention heads so each head covers a longer span of tokens
Replacing the softmax function in attention with a linear function to allow unbounded sequence length
Adding more transformer layers so stacked depth compensates for the shorter trained context length

items.rationale: Position Interpolation linearly rescales input position indices so that out-of-range positions are compressed back into the interpolation range RoPE was trained on, extending usable context with minimal fine-tuning. B conflates head count, a representational-capacity hyperparameter, with positional range, which it does not affect. C describes linear/kernelized attention variants (a different research line, e.g., Performer), not the positional-rescaling technique asked about. D confuses model depth with sequence-length handling; adding layers does not change how position indices are interpreted.

source.title: Chen et al., 'Extending Context Window of Large Language Models via Positional Interpolation', 2023

items.stem: In LLM literature, "hallucination" refers most precisely to

options.text: generated content that is fluent and plausible-sounding but factually incorrect or unsupported by the input or world knowledge
any output that a user subjectively finds unhelpful or poorly phrased
a training failure in which the loss diverges during pretraining
output that contains grammatical errors or broken syntax

items.rationale: Hallucination is specifically the generation of fluent, confident content that is factually wrong or unsupported by source/context, not merely low-quality output. B is too broad, conflating subjective dissatisfaction with factual fabrication. C confuses hallucination with an unrelated optimization failure (loss divergence) during training. D is a near-opposite trap: hallucinated text is typically grammatically well-formed and fluent, which is precisely what makes it dangerous; grammatical error is a distinct, unrelated failure mode.

source.title: Ji et al., 'Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation', ACM Computing Surveys, 2023

items.stem: BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and GPT (Radford et al., 2018) both build on the transformer but differ in a key architectural choice affecting how each token can attend to context. What is that difference?

options.text: BERT uses bidirectional self-attention over the full input via a masked-language-modeling objective, while GPT uses causal self-attention that masks future tokens
BERT uses causal self-attention while GPT uses bidirectional self-attention
BERT and GPT both use identical bidirectional attention, differing only in the number of layers
GPT uses an encoder-decoder architecture with cross-attention, while BERT uses decoder-only attention

items.rationale: BERT's masked-language-modeling pretraining lets tokens attend both left and right; GPT's autoregressive objective requires causal masking so a token cannot see future tokens. B is the exact reverse of the real distinction. C denies the actual architectural difference and misattributes it to layer count, which is not the defining contrast. D misassigns encoder-decoder-with-cross-attention (closer to the original Transformer or T5) to GPT and decoder-only to BERT, when in fact BERT is encoder-only and GPT is decoder-only.

source.title: Devlin et al., 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding', NAACL 2019; Radford et al., 'Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training', 2018

items.stem: Wei et al. (2022) reported that certain capabilities, such as multi-step arithmetic reasoning, appear only once model scale (parameters or training compute) crosses a certain threshold, and are largely absent in smaller models trained with the same data and objective. What is this phenomenon called?

options.text: Emergent abilities
Catastrophic forgetting
Overfitting
Knowledge distillation

items.rationale: The paper defines emergent abilities as capabilities not present in smaller models that appear discontinuously once scale crosses a threshold. B names an unrelated phenomenon: the loss of previously learned tasks during sequential training. C refers to fitting training data at the expense of generalization, an unrelated failure mode with no necessary connection to scale thresholds. D is a model-compression technique (transferring behavior from a teacher to a smaller student model), unrelated to scale-dependent capability emergence.

source.title: Wei et al., 'Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models', TMLR 2022

items.stem: Wei et al. (2022, 'Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models') found that prompting an LLM with a few exemplars containing intermediate reasoning steps, rather than input-output pairs alone, had what effect on multi-step reasoning tasks compared to standard few-shot prompting?

options.text: Substantially improved accuracy on tasks such as arithmetic and commonsense reasoning, with the benefit emerging mainly in large-scale models
No measurable difference in accuracy at any model scale, since reasoning ability is independent of prompt format
Improved accuracy uniformly across models of all sizes, including small models, at the same rate
Decreased accuracy, because longer prompts introduce more opportunities for the model to make errors

items.rationale: Chain-of-thought prompting produced large accuracy gains on reasoning benchmarks, but the paper shows this benefit is itself an emergent property, appearing mainly in sufficiently large models and negligible or even unhelpful in small ones. B directly contradicts the paper's central positive result. C overstates the finding by claiming uniform benefit across all scales, ignoring the scale-dependence that is the paper's key nuance (a trap mirroring the emergent-abilities theme). D encodes a converse error, the opposite of the observed improvement.

source.title: Wei et al., 'Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models', NeurIPS 2022

items.stem: A team adds one new input feature to a fraud-detection model and retrains it. The label distribution and all other features are unchanged, yet the calibration of five other, seemingly unrelated model outputs shifts noticeably after deployment. Which phenomenon, identified as a major source of hidden technical debt in production ML systems, best explains this?

options.text: CACE (Changing Anything Changes Everything): tight entanglement among features and model components means altering one input can shift the behavior of the whole system
Concept drift: the statistical relationship between inputs and the target has changed over time
Data cascades: an undetected upstream data-quality defect is propagating silently through the pipeline
Feedback loops: the model's own past predictions are altering the distribution of future training data

items.rationale: The scenario is a single, deliberate feature addition causing entanglement across unrelated outputs — the textbook CACE effect described by Sculley et al. (A). Concept drift (B) is a converse-style trap: it requires the input-output relationship to change over time due to the environment, not due to an engineering change made by the team. Data cascades (C) refer to undetected upstream data errors, not an intentional, known code change. Feedback loops (D) require the model's predictions to feed back into its own training data, which is not described here; it names a real but unrelated technical-debt category from the same paper.

source.title: Sculley, D. et al., "Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems," NeurIPS 2015

items.stem: A deployed credit-scoring model's accuracy degrades after a marketing campaign shifts the demographic mix of applicants. Analysis confirms that the true relationship between applicant features and default outcome (P(y|x)) has not changed; only the distribution of incoming feature values (P(x)) has shifted. In standard MLOps monitoring taxonomy, this is best classified as which type of drift?

options.text: Data drift (covariate shift): the input feature distribution has changed while the input-output relationship remains stable
Concept drift: the mapping between inputs and the target label has changed
Label drift: the marginal distribution of the target variable has shifted independently of the inputs
Training-serving skew: the feature engineering logic differs between the training and serving pipelines

items.rationale: Because P(x) shifted while P(y|x) stayed fixed, this is data/covariate drift (A). Concept drift (B) is the converse case — it applies when P(y|x) itself changes, which the scenario explicitly rules out. Label drift (C) describes a shift in P(y) alone and is not evidenced here (no information about default rate change is given). Training-serving skew (D) is a pipeline implementation bug causing systematic feature mismatch between training and serving code, not a shift in the real-world population over time.

source.title: Google Cloud, "MLOps: Continuous delivery and automation pipelines in machine learning"

items.stem: An organization has several teams independently recomputing similar customer features for different models, causing inconsistencies between the feature values used at training time and those computed at low-latency online inference time. Which MLOps component is purpose-built to provide a single, consistent, low-latency source of feature values shared across training and serving?

options.text: A feature store
A model registry
An experiment tracking system
A general-purpose data warehouse

items.rationale: A feature store (A) exists specifically to compute features once, store them centrally, and serve identical values to both training pipelines and low-latency online inference, eliminating training-serving skew and duplicate engineering. A model registry (B) versions and manages trained model artifacts and their deployment stage, not feature values. An experiment tracking system (C) logs hyperparameters, metrics, and run metadata, not shared feature computation. A data warehouse (D) is an overstated-scope distractor: it stores data at scale but offers no online low-latency serving path or training/serving consistency guarantee.

source.title: Uber Engineering, "Meet Michelangelo: Uber's Machine Learning Platform"

items.stem: A platform team needs a central system that stores versioned trained-model artifacts, records lineage (training data snapshot, code commit, hyperparameters), and tracks which version is currently tagged "staging" versus "production" so deployment pipelines can query it programmatically. Which MLOps component provides this capability?

options.text: A model registry
A feature store
A generic container image registry
A CI build server

items.rationale: A model registry (A), as implemented by tools such as MLflow Model Registry, is designed exactly for versioning artifacts, capturing lineage, and tracking deployment stage transitions. A feature store (B) manages feature values, not model artifacts or stage metadata. A generic container image registry (C) is an overstated-scope trap: it stores build artifacts (images) but has no concept of model lineage, hyperparameters, or staging/production stage tags. A CI build server (D) automates build and test pipelines but does not itself persist versioned model lineage or serve as the stage-of-record.

items.stem: A researcher reruns an identical training script with the identical config and gets identical model weights on their own machine. A colleague runs the same code and config on a different cluster and gets a materially different model. Investigation traces this to non-deterministic GPU operation ordering and unpinned library versions across the two environments. This gap is best described as a failure of which MLOps property?

options.text: Reproducibility: the ability to obtain the same result from the same code, data, and configuration regardless of environment
Portability: the ability to run the same pipeline across different infrastructure
Observability: the ability to inspect a system's internal state and behavior from its outputs
Scalability: the ability of a system to handle increasing data or traffic volume

items.rationale: Reproducibility (A) specifically requires that identical code, data, and configuration yield identical (or acceptably close) results across runs and environments; unpinned dependencies and non-determinism are classic reproducibility failures documented in production ML pipeline work such as TFX. Portability (B) is a related but distinct property — that the pipeline can execute at all across environments — and is not violated here since it did run, just with different results. Observability (C) concerns visibility into metrics/logs/traces, not numerical determinism of outputs. Scalability (D) concerns handling load, which is unrelated to the described discrepancy.

source.title: Baylor, D. et al., "TFX: A TensorFlow-Based Production-Scale Machine Learning Platform," KDD 2017

items.stem: A team wants to validate a new recommendation model against live production traffic before it can affect any user experience or business metric. They route a copy of every real production request to the new model in parallel, log its predictions for offline comparison against the current model, but never return its output to users. Which deployment pattern is this?

options.text: Shadow deployment: the new model scores live traffic silently in parallel without influencing served responses
Canary deployment: a small percentage of real users are actually served the new model's output
Blue-green deployment: traffic is instantly cut over from one fully live environment to another
A/B test: users are split into groups and shown different model outputs to compare business metrics statistically

items.rationale: Shadow deployment (A) is defined precisely by running the challenger model on real traffic while its outputs are logged, not served, so it cannot affect any user — matching the scenario exactly. Canary (B) is a converse-style trap: it does expose a subset of real users to the new model's actual output, which the scenario explicitly excludes. Blue-green (C) involves an instantaneous full cutover between two live environments, not silent parallel scoring. A/B testing (D) requires users to be split and actually shown differing outputs for statistical comparison, which contradicts "never returned to users."

source.title: Sato, D., Wider, A., Windheuser, C., "Continuous Delivery for Machine Learning," martinfowler.com, 2019

items.stem: An online marketplace tests a new ranking model by randomizing at the individual search-query level rather than per-user, then measures overall session conversion rate. As a result, the same user can see control-ranked results on one query and treatment-ranked results on the next within a single session. Which methodological problem does this randomization design most directly risk?

options.text: Violation of SUTVA (stable unit treatment value assumption) via interference between the treatment and control experiences within the same user's session
Insufficient statistical power caused by too small a sample size
Confounding introduced by an unmeasured pre-treatment variable correlated with assignment
Regression to the mean in the measured conversion metric across test periods

items.rationale: Randomizing at the query level while measuring a session-level outcome lets one user's control and treatment exposures interact and contaminate each other, violating SUTVA through interference/spillover — the correct diagnosis (A). Power (B) is a sample-size concern unrelated to the unit-of-randomization mismatch described. Confounding from a lurking variable (C) concerns omitted-variable bias in assignment, not interference between arms already assigned by randomization. Regression to the mean (D) is a distinct statistical artifact arising from selecting extreme observations, not from randomization granularity.

source.title: Kohavi, R., Tang, D., Xu, Y., "Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments: A Practical Guide to A/B Testing," Cambridge University Press, 2020

items.stem: A company trains models manually in notebooks and manually copies the resulting artifacts to production, with no automated pipeline for retraining and no continuous integration or delivery of pipeline components. According to Google Cloud's published MLOps maturity model, which level is this organization operating at?

options.text: MLOps level 0 — manual process
MLOps level 1 — ML pipeline automation
MLOps level 2 — CI/CD pipeline automation
DevOps level 2 maturity

items.rationale: Level 0 (A) is defined by exactly this profile: manual, script-driven, notebook-based training with manual deployment and no automated retraining or CI/CD. Level 1 (B) requires an automated, continuously retraining ML pipeline, which is explicitly absent here — choosing it overstates the described maturity. Level 2 (C) additionally requires CI/CD automation of the pipeline components themselves, the highest maturity tier, which is the opposite error. Option D is a sibling-framework trap: it substitutes the generic DevOps maturity model for the MLOps-specific one the scenario is being evaluated against, and no such "DevOps level 2" designation applies here.

source.title: Google Cloud, "MLOps: Continuous delivery and automation pipelines in machine learning"

items.stem: A fraud-detection team trains a binary classifier on transaction data where confirmed fraud makes up 0.2% of cases. The deployed model reports 99.8% test accuracy but its precision-recall behavior turns out to be close to a majority-class classifier that flags almost no fraud. Which single summary metric, computed on the same confusion matrix before deployment, would most reliably have exposed this problem?

options.text: Matthews correlation coefficient
Overall accuracy recomputed on a larger held-out sample
ROC-AUC computed on the same test set
Top-1 error rate as used in image-classification benchmarks

items.rationale: MCC uses all four confusion-matrix cells (TP, TN, FP, FN) in a single correlation-style score and collapses toward zero for a classifier that is really just predicting the majority class, regardless of class prevalence (Chicco & Jurman, 2020, BMC Genomics, 'The advantages of the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) over F1 score and accuracy in binary classification evaluation'). B just re-measures the same accuracy statistic that produced the paradox — a larger sample does not fix the base-rate distortion. C is the classic converse trap: with 1:500 imbalance, ROC-AUC can remain deceptively high because the false-positive rate axis is normalized by the huge negative class and barely moves even when precision collapses (Saito & Rehmsmeier, 2015, PLOS ONE). D is scope-mismatched: top-1 error is a multiclass benchmark convention (e.g., ImageNet), not a metric for a binary imbalanced decision problem.

source.title: Saito & Rehmsmeier (2015), The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than ROC on Imbalanced Data, PLOS ONE

items.stem: Two rare-disease screening models (prevalence about 1%) both show ROC-AUC near 0.95. When the team instead plots precision-recall curves on the same test set, one model's PR-AUC drops to 0.35 while the other's stays near 0.80. What explains why the PR curves revealed a gap that the ROC curves did not?

options.text: The false-positive rate axis in ROC space is normalized by a very large negative class, so a real increase in false positives barely shifts the ROC curve, while it sharply reduces precision
ROC-AUC and PR-AUC are mathematically equivalent on any dataset, so the discrepancy indicates a bug in the PR-curve computation
PR curves are automatically re-normalized for class balance, so the 0.80 vs. 0.35 gap is an artifact introduced by that normalization step
ROC-AUC is statistically undefined once class prevalence drops below about 5%, so the reported 0.95 values carry no meaning

items.rationale: Under heavy imbalance, FPR = FP/(FP+TN) changes very little as FP grows because TN is huge, so ROC curves stay flattering; precision = TP/(TP+FP) is directly sensitive to the same false positives and drops sharply — this is the documented reason PR curves are more informative than ROC curves on skewed data (Davis & Goadrich, 2006, ICML, 'The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves'; Saito & Rehmsmeier, 2015). B is a converse error: the two curves are related but not equivalent, and they diverge precisely under imbalance. C invents a fictitious 'normalization artifact' — PR curves are not renormalized for class balance, they are simply computed from precision, which is itself sensitive to it. D overstates scope: ROC-AUC remains well-defined at any prevalence, it is just less discriminating, not meaningless.

source.title: Davis & Goadrich (2006), The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves, ICML

items.stem: A loan-default model keeps a high AUC-ROC after deployment, but its predicted probabilities in the 0.7-0.9 range systematically overstate the actual observed default rate for those cases. Which evaluation approach is designed to directly detect this specific failure, since ranking-based discrimination metrics like AUC do not?

options.text: Reliability diagrams and expected calibration error (ECE)
A larger k in k-fold cross-validation to stabilize the AUC estimate
Macro-averaged F1 computed separately within each predicted-risk decile
Bootstrap confidence intervals around the point estimate of AUC

items.rationale: Reliability diagrams bin predictions by predicted probability and compare them to observed frequencies; ECE summarizes the gap, which is exactly what is needed to detect systematic over- or under-confidence that ranking metrics ignore (Guo, Pleiss, Sun & Weinberger, 2017, ICML, 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks'). B addresses estimator variance of AUC, not calibration, and AUC is insensitive to probability accuracy by construction. C is still a discrimination-style metric (based on thresholded class decisions), not a measure of whether the probability values themselves are accurate. D quantifies uncertainty in the AUC point estimate, which says nothing about the systematic bias described in the stem.

source.title: Guo et al. (2017), On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks, ICML

items.stem: A team has only 300 held-out examples to recalibrate a boosted-tree classifier. Its reliability diagram shows a smooth, monotonic sigmoidal distortion typical of max-margin-style models. Given both the shape of the miscalibration and the small validation set, which post-hoc calibration method is most appropriate?

options.text: Platt scaling (a single logistic regression fit on the classifier's scores)
Isotonic regression
Temperature scaling with a separate temperature parameter per class
Histogram binning with 50 equal-frequency bins

items.rationale: Platt scaling fits only two parameters and is a natural match for the smooth sigmoidal miscalibration common to boosted trees and SVM-like scores, and its low parameter count makes it stable with as few as a few hundred calibration points (Platt, 1999; Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 2005, ICML, 'Predicting Good Probabilities With Supervised Learning'). B, isotonic regression, is non-parametric and needs substantially more calibration data than 300 points to avoid overfitting the calibration map itself — a well-documented trap in the same paper. C misapplies temperature scaling: Guo et al. (2017) define it with a single scalar temperature for multiclass neural network logits, not a per-class vector, and it is not the appropriate tool here either way. D, with 50 bins over 300 points, yields roughly six examples per bin — far too sparse to estimate per-bin frequencies reliably.

source.title: Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana (2005), Predicting Good Probabilities with Supervised Learning, ICML

items.stem: A genomics team selects the 100 most differentially expressed genes using the full dataset, labels included, and then runs 10-fold cross-validation restricted to those 100 features, reporting 95% CV accuracy. On a genuinely independent external cohort, the same classifier scores only 58%. What most likely explains the gap?

options.text: Feature selection was performed on the whole dataset before cross-validation, so label information from every fold leaked into the feature set that CV later evaluated
The external cohort had a different disease prevalence, which changes accuracy independently of how the internal CV was conducted
10-fold cross-validation is inherently more optimistic than 5-fold cross-validation
The classifier's model family was too simple to generalize beyond the training cohort

items.rationale: Because gene selection used the full label set before any fold was held out, each CV test fold's labels had already influenced which features were kept, inflating the CV estimate — the canonical selection-bias mechanism (Ambroise & McLachlan, 2002, PNAS, 'Selection bias in gene extraction on the basis of microarray gene-expression data'). B deflects the explanation onto an unrelated dataset-shift story that does not address the leakage mechanism actually described. C is a false general claim; the fold count itself is not a source of optimistic bias of this magnitude. D misdiagnoses the cause as model capacity, which the stem gives no evidence for, ignoring the described feature-selection procedure.

source.title: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning, §7.10.2 (the wrong way to do cross-validation)

items.stem: Researchers tune hyperparameters via grid search using a single 5-fold cross-validation loop, pick the configuration with the best mean CV score, and report that same CV score as the expected generalization performance. An independent test set later shows meaningfully worse performance. What methodological change would have produced an unbiased performance estimate during model selection?

options.text: Nested cross-validation, with an outer loop estimating performance and an inner loop performing hyperparameter selection
Repeating the same single 5-fold CV several times with different random seeds and averaging the results
Increasing the number of folds from 5 to 10 within the same single CV loop
Replacing grid search with random search over the same hyperparameter space

items.rationale: Nested CV separates the data used to choose hyperparameters (inner loop) from the data used to estimate the final model's performance (outer loop), removing the optimism that comes from selecting the best-scoring configuration on the same folds later reported as the estimate (Cawley & Talbot, 2010, JMLR, 'On Over-fitting in Model Selection and Subsequent Selection Bias in Performance Evaluation'). B reduces variance from resampling noise but does not address the structural bias of reusing one CV score for both selection and reporting. C changes fold granularity, not the fact that the same score is used twice for two different purposes. D changes the search strategy over hyperparameters but leaves the selection-then-report leakage fully intact.

source.title: Cawley & Talbot (2010), On Over-fitting in Model Selection and Subsequent Selection Bias, JMLR

items.stem: A quant team predicts next-day stock returns and evaluates the model using standard k-fold cross-validation with random shuffling of all trading days across folds. Live backtested performance turns out far worse than the CV estimate suggested. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Random shuffling placed days adjacent in time into different folds, letting the model exploit temporal autocorrelation and information not actually available at prediction time
The model overfit because it used too many features relative to the number of training examples
The individual test folds were too small to produce a statistically reliable accuracy estimate
Stock returns are non-stationary, so no cross-validation procedure could ever produce a valid performance estimate for this task

items.rationale: Randomly shuffled k-fold CV ignores time order, so a training fold can contain days that are temporally close to (even immediately surrounding) a test-fold day, letting the model pick up on autocorrelated structure it would never have at true prediction time; the standard fix is walk-forward or blocked time-series CV that respects chronology. B is a generic overfitting story that does not explain why CV specifically overstated performance rather than simply being poor across the board. C is a sample-size complaint, not a leakage explanation, and does not account for the systematic direction of the gap. D overstates the case — properly time-ordered (walk-forward/blocked) CV can produce valid, if noisier, estimates for non-stationary series; the problem here is the shuffling, not an inherent impossibility.

source.title: Bergmeir & Benítez (2012), On the use of cross-validation for time series predictor evaluation, Information Sciences

items.stem: A hospital team splits chest X-ray images into training and test folds at the image level, even though most patients contributed several images each. Internal test AUC is 0.97, but the same model scores only 0.75 AUC on a different hospital's images. Which data-splitting mistake does this describe?

options.text: Group leakage from splitting at the image level, so images from the same patient appear in both training and test folds
Class imbalance between the internal training set and the external hospital's population
An internal test set too small to yield a statistically stable AUC
Covariate shift caused by the external hospital using different scanner hardware

items.rationale: Splitting by image rather than by patient allows the model to memorize patient-specific artifacts (anatomy, positioning, device quirks) shared across images from the same person, inflating internal test performance without reflecting genuine generalization — the canonical 'leakage via grouped data' failure (Kaufman, Rosset, Perlich & Stitelman, 2012, ACM TKDD, 'Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, and Avoidance'); the fix is patient-level (grouped) splitting. B redirects to a prevalence-shift story that is unrelated to the same-patient overlap mechanism actually described. C is a variance complaint that does not explain the systematic direction and size of the internal/external gap. D names a real phenomenon in domain generalization, but it does not explain the described overlap-driven mechanism — the leakage is the correctly identifiable cause here, not merely a shift in imaging hardware.

source.title: Kaufman et al. (2012), Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, and Avoidance, ACM TKDD

items.stem: An LLM developer reports strong scores on a widely used QA benchmark. External researchers later show the model can regenerate several benchmark test questions verbatim when prompted with only their first few words, indicating the benchmark text was likely present in pretraining data. Which term specifically names this problem?

options.text: Benchmark (data) contamination
Catastrophic forgetting
Overfitting to the validation set through repeated hyperparameter tuning
Distribution shift between the benchmark and the deployment environment

items.rationale: Benchmark contamination refers to test data appearing in a model's pretraining corpus, inflating reported performance without genuine task capability; it is explicitly discussed and measured in the GPT-3 paper's contamination analysis (Brown et al., 2020, 'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners', Appendix C) and formalized further in later work (Sainz et al., 2023, EMNLP, 'NLP Evaluation in Trouble'). B names an unrelated phenomenon — loss of previously learned capabilities during continued training or fine-tuning, not memorization of test items. C is the classical supervised-learning leakage concept from repeated validation-set reuse during tuning, not the pretraining-corpus memorization described here. D describes a mismatch between benchmark and deployment populations, not verbatim reproduction of exact test items, which is a memorization problem rather than a population mismatch.

source.title: Sainz et al. (2023), NLP Evaluation in Trouble: On the Need to Measure LLM Data Contamination, EMNLP Findings

items.stem: To respond to contamination critiques of a new LLM's benchmark scores, evaluators search for n-gram or substring overlap between the model's pretraining corpus and each benchmark's test examples, then report scores separately for the overlapping and non-overlapping subsets. Which established practice does this describe?

options.text: n-gram/substring overlap decontamination analysis
Held-out cross-validation
Bootstrapped confidence intervals around the benchmark accuracy
Adversarial paraphrase testing of the benchmark questions

items.rationale: Searching pretraining corpora for overlapping n-grams or substrings with benchmark test items, then reporting 'clean' versus 'dirty' subset scores, is the decontamination methodology introduced in the GPT-3 paper's Appendix C (Brown et al., 2020) and later formalized with black-box detection tests (Oren et al., 2023, 'Proving Test Set Contamination in Black-Box Language Models'; Sainz et al., 2023). B is a resampling technique for estimating generalization from a fixed labeled dataset — it has no mechanism for checking whether pretraining data overlaps with a fixed external benchmark. C quantifies sampling uncertainty in an accuracy estimate and says nothing about whether the model memorized test content. D tests robustness to wording changes in the questions, which probes generalization but does not detect verbatim overlap between pretraining data and benchmark text.

source.title: Yang et al. (2023), Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples

items.stem: A data engineering team runs an automated data quality check on a training dataset for a fraud-detection model. The check flags every record where the transaction_country field is blank. Per the DAMA-DMBOK data quality dimensions, a blank required field is a violation of which dimension?

options.text: Completeness
Consistency
Timeliness
Validity

items.rationale: Completeness measures whether required data is present at all; a blank mandatory field is the textbook completeness failure (DAMA-DMBOK2, Ch.13). Consistency (B) concerns agreement of a value across systems or records, not absence of a value. Timeliness (C) concerns whether data arrives within the needed window, unrelated to missing values. Validity (D) concerns whether a present value conforms to a defined domain or format (e.g., an invalid country code like 'XX') - a plausible trap, but it presumes a value exists to check against a rule, which is not the case here.

source.title: DAMA-DMBOK: Data Management Body of Knowledge, 2nd Edition (DAMA International, 2017), Ch. 13 Data Quality

items.stem: A Kafka-based feature pipeline stores Avro-encoded events, with Confluent Schema Registry compatibility mode set to BACKWARD. The team adds a new field, device_type, to the event schema. They need consumers that later upgrade to the new schema to still be able to read events already stored under the old schema. What must the new field definition include for this to hold?

options.text: A default value specified for the new field
No default value, since Avro fills gaps automatically
A switch of the registry mode to FORWARD before the change is registered
Removal of an unrelated existing field to keep the record size constant

items.rationale: Under BACKWARD compatibility, a schema that adds a field remains compatible only if that field carries a default, so that a consumer reading old data (which lacks the field entirely) under the new schema can populate it from the default (Confluent Schema Registry docs, 'Schema Evolution and Compatibility'). B is the direct misconception being tested: Avro does not auto-fill values, the default must be declared. C confuses FORWARD (new data read by old schema) with the BACKWARD direction actually in play here; switching modes does not change Avro's resolution rules. D is an irrelevant, unmotivated change that does not address the missing-field problem.

source.title: Confluent Documentation: Schema Evolution and Compatibility for Schema Registry

items.stem: A data engineering team currently runs a Lambda architecture for feature computation: a batch layer recomputes historical features nightly, and a separate speed layer computes real-time approximations that are later reconciled against the batch results. They migrate to a Kappa architecture instead. As originally proposed by Jay Kreps, what is the defining structural change in this migration?

options.text: Replacing the batch and speed layers with a single stream-processing pipeline that reprocesses history by replaying the log
Adding a third reconciliation layer that merges batch and speed-layer outputs in real time
Replacing the append-only log with a nightly batch dump to reduce end-to-end latency
Running the existing batch layer and speed layer on the same physical cluster to cut infrastructure cost

items.rationale: Kappa architecture eliminates the batch layer entirely: all processing, including reprocessing of historical data, is done by a single stream processor replaying the retained log from the desired offset (Kreps, 'Questioning the Lambda Architecture', 2014). B inverts the point of Kappa, which removes the need for a reconciliation layer rather than adding one. C reverses the direction of change: moving to a batch dump is a step toward Lambda, not away from it. D describes co-locating two layers rather than eliminating one, missing the architectural simplification Kappa is defined by.

source.title: Jay Kreps, 'Questioning the Lambda Architecture', O'Reilly Radar, 2014

items.stem: An auditor needs to trace exactly which upstream source columns contributed to a single output feature, credit_risk_score, that was flagged as anomalous, without inspecting every table that the pipeline touched. Which lineage capability specifically supports this need?

options.text: Column-level (fine-grained) lineage
Table-level lineage
Operational metadata logging of job start and end times
Schema registry version history

items.rationale: Column-level lineage tracks transformations at the attribute level, letting an auditor trace one output field back to its specific contributing source columns (OpenLineage specification; DAMA-DMBOK2 Ch.9 on lineage). Table-level lineage (B) only records which tables fed which other tables, not which columns within them - an overstated-granularity trap for this need. Operational metadata (C) records job execution timing, a distinct metadata category from data-flow lineage. Schema registry history (D) tracks how a schema's structure changed over time, not how data flowed between columns.

items.stem: A team wants reproducible ML training: given a commit hash, they must be able to reconstruct the exact dataset, including files that were later deleted, without duplicating unchanged files on every version. Which data versioning approach, as implemented by tools such as DVC or lakeFS, achieves this?

options.text: Content-addressable storage, where a manifest per version points to immutable file or object hashes
Overwriting the dataset in place while maintaining a separate human-written changelog document
Storing every historical row physically duplicated in a full daily snapshot file
Relying solely on a database transaction log, with no external manifest of stored objects

items.rationale: DVC and lakeFS both version data by hashing file or object content and storing a lightweight manifest (a .dvc file or commit metadata) that maps a version to a set of immutable, content-addressed blobs; unchanged files are referenced, not recopied (DVC documentation, 'Data and Model Versioning'; lakeFS documentation, 'lakeFS Concepts'). B substitutes a narrative changelog for content-addressing, which cannot reconstruct exact byte-for-byte state or deduplicate storage. C is the storage-explosion outcome that content-addressable versioning is specifically designed to avoid. D describes table-level transaction-log versioning (as in Delta Lake) without the manifest of content-addressed objects needed for large-file deduplication and exact reconstruction described in the stem.

source.title: DVC Documentation: Data and Model Versioning

items.stem: A team synchronizes a feature store with an operational Postgres database by having application code write to both Postgres and the feature store within the same request handler, without a distributed transaction. Following Kleppmann's analysis in Designing Data-Intensive Applications, what is the core reliability problem with this dual-write approach relative to log-based change data capture?

options.text: If one write succeeds and the other fails, the two systems can permanently diverge with no defined ordering guarantee
Dual writes always saturate network bandwidth because every write is duplicated in transit
Dual writes prevent schema evolution because both systems must share a single schema file
Dual writes require the feature store to support SQL joins identical to Postgres

items.rationale: Kleppmann's DDIA (Ch. 11) identifies the core dual-write hazard as partial failure and race conditions: without a shared transaction or ordering mechanism, the two systems can end up inconsistent with each other, whereas log-based CDC provides a single ordered source of truth both systems derive from. B overstates the concern as a bandwidth problem, which DDIA does not raise as the core issue. C is unrelated - schema evolution problems are independent of whether writes are dual or CDC-based. D misattributes a query-capability requirement that has no bearing on write consistency.

source.title: Martin Kleppmann, Designing Data-Intensive Applications, O'Reilly Media, 2017, Chapter 11: Stream Processing

items.stem: A retail demand-forecasting feature pipeline ingests daily sales totals correctly and without error, but the data is not delivered to the training pipeline until 36 hours after end of business, past the required daily refresh window. Per DAMA-DMBOK, which data quality dimension is violated, even though the values themselves are correct?

options.text: Timeliness
Accuracy
Uniqueness
Integrity

items.rationale: Timeliness measures whether data is available when it is needed for its intended use; late-but-correct data is a timeliness failure (DAMA-DMBOK2, Ch.13). Accuracy (B) concerns whether values correctly represent the real-world state - here they do, so accuracy is not violated, making this the intended trap of conflating 'late' with 'wrong'. Uniqueness (C) concerns duplicate records, which is not described. Integrity (D) concerns structural or referential relationships between data elements, not delivery scheduling.

source.title: DAMA-DMBOK: Data Management Body of Knowledge, 2nd Edition (DAMA International, 2017), Ch. 13 Data Quality

items.stem: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, published January 2023) structures AI risk management activities into four core functions that organizations are meant to apply iteratively across the AI lifecycle. Which list correctly names these four functions?

options.text: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage
Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond
Plan, Do, Check, Act
Design, Develop, Deploy, Decommission

items.rationale: AI RMF 1.0 (NIST, Jan. 2023) defines exactly four core functions — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage — with Govern acting as a cross-cutting function informing the other three. (B) names the four functions of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF), a sibling NIST framework for a different risk domain, not the AI RMF. (C) is the generic ISO 9001/PDCA quality-management cycle, not a NIST AI RMF construct. (D) is a generic AI lifecycle description invented for this item, not the RMF's named functions.

items.stem: An engineer generates x_adv = x + ε·sign(∇ₓ J(θ, x, y)) from a correctly classified image x, adding a perturbation that is imperceptible to a human but reliably flips the model's prediction. This single-step attack method is known as...

options.text: the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM), introduced by Goodfellow et al. (2014)
Projected Gradient Descent (PGD), introduced by Madry et al. (2017)
the Carlini–Wagner (C&W) attack, introduced by Carlini & Wagner (2017)
DeepFool, introduced by Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2016)

items.rationale: The formula x+ε·sign(∇J) is FGSM's exact defining equation from Goodfellow, Shlens & Szegedy (2014), 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples.' (B) PGD iterates a similar sign-gradient step multiple times with projection back onto an ε-ball and random restarts — a stronger multi-step attack, not the single-step formula shown. (C) C&W formulates an optimization over a margin-based loss with no ε·sign step at all, and is generally stronger than FGSM. (D) DeepFool iteratively linearizes the decision boundary to find a minimal perturbation, using no sign-of-gradient step.

source.title: Goodfellow, Shlens & Szegedy, "Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples" (2014)

items.stem: A fraud-detection model trained on 2019–2021 transaction data experiences a marked change in the distribution of input features P(X) after a new payment platform launches in 2024 — new merchant categories, new device fingerprints — while the underlying relationship between features and the fraud label, P(Y|X), stays the same. Per the standard dataset-shift taxonomy, this is best classified as...

options.text: covariate shift
concept drift
prior probability (label) shift
domain adaptation failure

items.rationale: Per Quiñonero-Candela et al. (2009), Dataset Shift in Machine Learning, covariate shift is defined as a change in P(X) with P(Y|X) unchanged — exactly the scenario described. (B) Concept drift is the converse case: P(Y|X) itself changes over time (the meaning of the fraud pattern shifts), which the stem explicitly rules out. (C) Prior probability shift refers to a change in P(Y) with P(X|Y) stable, a label-frequency change, not a feature-distribution change. (D) 'Domain adaptation failure' names a downstream consequence, not a recognized shift-type category in the taxonomy.

source.title: Quiñonero-Candela, Sugiyama, Schwaighofer & Lawrence, "Dataset Shift in Machine Learning" (MIT Press, 2009)

items.stem: NIST AI 100-2e2023 ('Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology of Attacks and Mitigations') describes an attack in which an adversary manipulates a small fraction of the training data to implant a hidden trigger pattern; the trained model behaves normally on clean inputs but misclassifies any input containing the trigger at inference time. This attack is classified as a...

options.text: backdoor (poisoning) attack
evasion attack
membership inference attack
model extraction attack

items.rationale: NIST AI 100-2e2023 classifies attacks that corrupt training data to implant a trigger as backdoor/poisoning attacks — training-time manipulation and trigger-conditioned misbehavior are its defining features. (B) Evasion attacks perturb test-time inputs against a model whose training data was never touched, the reverse of what is described. (C) Membership inference is a privacy attack inferring whether a specific record was in the training set, unrelated to inducing misclassification. (D) Model extraction is a privacy/IP attack that reconstructs a model's functionality via query access, not a misclassification-inducing attack.

source.title: NIST AI 100-2e2023, "Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology of Attacks and Mitigations"

items.stem: Guo et al. (2017) showed that modern deep neural networks are frequently overconfident: a model reporting 90% confidence for a batch of predictions is correct far less than 90% of the time. To correct this on a trained classifier without retraining or changing its accuracy, they recommend rescaling logits before the softmax using a single learned scalar. This post-hoc method is called...

options.text: temperature scaling
Platt scaling
isotonic regression
histogram binning

items.rationale: Guo et al. (2017), 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks,' show temperature scaling — dividing logits by one learned scalar T before softmax — best corrects miscalibration in deep nets while leaving the arg-max prediction (and thus accuracy) unchanged. (B) Platt scaling fits a full logistic regression on the scores (more parameters than a single scalar) and is shown in the same paper to be outperformed by temperature scaling on multi-class deep networks. (C) Isotonic regression is a non-parametric monotonic mapping shown to be prone to overfitting relative to temperature scaling, especially with modest validation-set sizes. (D) Histogram binning is a coarser non-parametric method also shown to underperform temperature scaling in their experiments.

source.title: Guo, Pleiss, Sun & Weinberger, "On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks" (ICML 2017)

items.stem: A team wants predictive uncertainty estimates for a deployed classifier without altering the training loss or performing Bayesian inference over weights. They train M independently and randomly initialized copies of the same architecture on the same data and average the resulting predictive distributions at test time, using the spread across members as an uncertainty signal. This approach is known as...

options.text: deep ensembles
Monte Carlo dropout
variational Bayesian neural networks
temperature scaling

items.rationale: Lakshminarayanan, Pritzel & Blundell (2017), 'Simple and Scalable Predictive Uncertainty Estimation using Deep Ensembles,' describe exactly this recipe — independently initialized, independently trained copies whose disagreement signals uncertainty — and show it is competitive with or better than Bayesian alternatives. (B) Monte Carlo dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) obtains uncertainty from repeated stochastic forward passes with dropout on a single trained network, not from M independently trained networks. (C) Variational Bayesian neural networks estimate a posterior over weights by directly modifying the training objective (an ELBO), which the stem explicitly excludes. (D) Temperature scaling recalibrates a single model's confidence post-hoc; it produces no ensemble disagreement signal and does not by itself estimate epistemic uncertainty.

source.title: Lakshminarayanan, Pritzel & Blundell, "Simple and Scalable Predictive Uncertainty Estimation using Deep Ensembles" (NeurIPS 2017)

items.stem: Madry et al. (2017) framed adversarial training as a saddle-point problem: minimize over model parameters θ the expected value, over training data, of the maximum over perturbations δ within an ε-ball of the loss L(θ, x+δ, y). For a first-order adversary, which procedure did they identify as solving the inner maximization reliably enough to make the resulting min-max training robust to a wide range of attacks?

options.text: Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) with multiple steps and random restarts
the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM), a single gradient step
the Fast Gradient Method (FGM) under an L2 norm constraint
randomized smoothing with Gaussian noise

items.rationale: Madry et al. (2017), 'Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks,' identify PGD — a multi-step, projected first-order method with random restarts — as reliably approximating the inner maximum, making PGD-based adversarial training robust to essentially all first-order adversaries tested. (B) FGSM's single step is too weak an inner-maximization procedure: models adversarially trained only against FGSM overfit to it (a documented gradient-masking failure) and remain vulnerable to iterative attacks. (C) FGM under an L2 constraint is a variant used elsewhere for L2-bounded threat models, not the L∞ inner-maximization procedure Madry et al. designate as their near-universal first-order adversary. (D) Randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019) is a certification technique giving provable robustness guarantees; it is not an attack/inner-maximization procedure and postdates this paper.

source.title: Madry, Makelov, Schmidt, Tsipras & Vladu, "Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks" (ICLR 2018)

items.stem: A safety team wants to scale adversarial testing beyond what human red-teamers can manually produce. They train a separate 'red' language model whose objective is to generate prompts that make a target language model emit harmful or toxic outputs, then use the discovered failure cases to improve the target model. This automated red-teaming method, distinct from earlier efforts that relied on crowdworkers to hand-write adversarial prompts, was introduced in...

options.text: Perez et al. (2022), "Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models"
Ganguli et al. (2022), "Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms"
Wallace et al. (2019), "Universal Adversarial Triggers for Attacking and Analyzing NLP"
Bai et al. (2022), "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback"

items.rationale: Perez et al. (2022, DeepMind), 'Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models,' introduces exactly this pipeline: a generator LM automatically produces adversarial prompts to elicit harmful target-model outputs at scale, which are then used to improve the target model. (B) Ganguli et al. (2022, Anthropic) release a large-scale dataset and analysis of red-teaming, but their method centers on human (crowdworker) red-teamers writing the adversarial prompts — the manual approach the stem contrasts against — not LM-generated attacks. (C) Wallace et al. (2019) search for universal adversarial trigger tokens via gradient-guided search over a fixed vocabulary, an earlier, non-generative attack method aimed at NLP classifiers, not at eliciting harms from a target LM. (D) Bai et al. (2022) describes Anthropic's RLHF pipeline for helpfulness/harmlessness training; it is not a method for automatically generating adversarial red-team prompts.

source.title: Perez et al., "Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models" (EMNLP 2022)

items.stem: An attacker embeds malicious instructions inside a webpage that a browsing-enabled LLM agent later retrieves and processes as part of its context, causing the agent to exfiltrate user data — without the attacker ever submitting text directly to the model's chat interface. Which OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) category best describes this attack?

options.text: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection (indirect vector)
LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency
LLM09:2025 Misinformation
LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure

items.rationale: Correct: instructions arriving via a third-party data source (the webpage) rather than the user's direct input is the defining trait of indirect prompt injection under LLM01:2025. B is wrong because Excessive Agency concerns over-broad permissions/tools that amplify an attack's impact, not the injection vector itself. C is wrong because Misinformation covers model-generated false content, not adversarially injected attacker instructions. D is wrong because it names the consequence (data exfiltration) rather than the attack technique that caused it.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
