items.stem: Researchers train a binary classifier that takes a target model's prediction-confidence vector for a given input and outputs whether that input was a member of the target's training set, exploiting the fact that overfitted models respond with higher confidence to training samples than to unseen ones. Shokri et al. (2017) formalized this as:

options.text: A membership inference attack
A model inversion attack
A model extraction attack
A data poisoning attack

items.rationale: Correct: deciding training-set membership from confidence scores is precisely the membership inference attack Shokri et al. defined. B is wrong because model inversion reconstructs representative input features (e.g., an image) from outputs, not a binary in/out decision. C is wrong because model extraction recovers the target model's functionality/parameters, not facts about its training set. D is wrong because poisoning is a training-time integrity attack, whereas this is a post-training, inference-time privacy attack.

source.title: Shokri, Stronati, Song, Shmatikov — Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models (IEEE S&P 2017)

items.stem: An adversary with only black-box query access to a proprietary machine-learning-as-a-service API systematically submits inputs and uses the returned prediction probabilities to build a local model that closely approximates the target's decision boundaries and parameters, at a fraction of the original training cost. Tramèr et al. (2016) named this class of attack:

options.text: Model extraction (model stealing)
Membership inference
Model inversion
Adversarial example crafting

items.rationale: Correct: reconstructing a functionally equivalent model from query-response pairs is model extraction, as defined by Tramèr et al. B is wrong because membership inference determines whether specific records were in the training set, not whether the model's functionality can be duplicated. C is wrong because model inversion reconstructs sensitive training inputs, not the model's own parameters/decision boundary. D is wrong because adversarial example crafting perturbs inputs to fool a fixed target model at inference time; it does not steal the model.

source.title: Tramèr, Zhang, Juels, Reiter, Ristenpart — Stealing Machine Learning Models via Prediction APIs (USENIX Security 2016)

items.stem: Given only API access to a facial-recognition model and a target class label, an attacker iteratively optimizes an input image to maximize the model's confidence score for that class, eventually recovering an image that resembles an actual training-set subject's face. Fredrikson et al. (2015) classified this as:

options.text: A model inversion attack
A membership inference attack
A model extraction attack
An adversarial evasion attack

items.rationale: Correct: reconstructing a recognizable representation of a training input by optimizing against confidence output is the model inversion attack Fredrikson et al. described. B is wrong because membership inference only answers a binary in/out-of-training-set question and does not reconstruct the input itself. C is wrong because extraction targets the model's parameters/functionality, not sensitive training data. D is wrong because evasion attacks craft inputs to cause misclassification at test time; they do not aim to recover private training data.

source.title: Fredrikson, Jha, Ristenpart — Model Inversion Attacks that Exploit Confidence Information and Basic Countermeasures (ACM CCS 2015)

items.stem: A vendor curates a public image dataset by scraping the web. An adversary adds a small number of images whose labels remain correct and visually consistent under human review, but whose pixels are subtly perturbed so that the trained classifier systematically misclassifies inputs containing a specific trigger pattern at test time. This describes:

options.text: Clean-label data poisoning
Label-flipping poisoning
Membership inference
Model inversion

items.rationale: Correct: keeping labels correct and human-plausible while perturbing features to implant a targeted misclassification is the defining property of clean-label poisoning. B is wrong because label-flipping poisoning works by assigning incorrect labels, which would fail the human-review consistency check described. C is wrong because membership inference is an inference-time privacy attack on a trained model, not a training-time integrity attack on the dataset. D is wrong because model inversion reconstructs training data from a trained model rather than corrupting training data beforehand.

source.title: Biggio, Nelson, Laskov — Poisoning Attacks against Support Vector Machines (ICML 2012)

items.stem: A development team downloads a pretrained language model checkpoint from a public model hub and fine-tunes it for production use, without verifying the checkpoint's provenance or scanning its serialization format for embedded malicious code (e.g., unsafe pickle deserialization). This exposure falls under which OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) category?

options.text: LLM03:2025 Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning
LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency

items.rationale: Correct: risk arising from an untrusted, unverified third-party model artifact is precisely LLM03's supply-chain scope. B is wrong because Data and Model Poisoning concerns adversarial manipulation of training data or the training process itself, not the trustworthiness of a downloaded artifact. C is wrong because Vector and Embedding Weaknesses addresses retrieval-store/RAG risks, not pretrained-checkpoint provenance. D is wrong because Excessive Agency concerns runtime permissions granted to an agent's tools/actions, unrelated to upstream artifact trust.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM03:2025 Supply Chain

items.stem: An attacker sends a stream of crafted prompts that each induce an LLM application to recursively expand its own context — for example, via chained tool calls that re-feed prior outputs as new inputs — driving up compute cost and latency until the service becomes unavailable to legitimate users, without exploiting any network-layer flaw. Which OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) category covers this?

options.text: LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption
LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency
LLM09:2025 Misinformation

items.rationale: Correct: uncontrolled resource/compute exhaustion driven through legitimate model functionality is the scope of Unbounded Consumption. B is wrong because prompt injection is about hijacking the model's instructions/behavior, not about exhausting resources through repeated legitimate-looking requests. C is wrong because Excessive Agency concerns over-privileged tool/action scope, not the resource-exhaustion mechanism itself. D is wrong because Misinformation concerns fabricated or false output content, not availability degradation.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption

items.stem: The ENISA 'Artificial Intelligence Threat Landscape Report' (2020) organizes AI-specific threats according to which structuring principle?

options.text: Stages of the machine-learning pipeline (from business/data understanding through deployment) cross-referenced with ENISA's standard horizontal threat-taxonomy categories (nefarious activity/abuse, eavesdropping, physical attack, etc.)
Severity tiers aligned to the EU AI Act's risk-based classification (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal risk)
The CIA triad (confidentiality, integrity, availability) applied as the sole organizing axis for every AI asset
MITRE ATT&CK tactic categories adapted one-to-one from enterprise IT into ML pipelines

items.rationale: Correct: ENISA's report maps assets and threats across ML-pipeline lifecycle stages and cross-references them with its pre-existing horizontal threat taxonomy. B is wrong because the AI Act's risk tiers are a distinct, later regulatory classification scheme, not ENISA's threat-mapping structure — a sibling-framework confusion. C is wrong because the CIA triad is a generic security lens ENISA discusses but does not use as the sole structuring principle; this overstates its scope in the report. D is wrong because adapting ATT&CK tactics one-to-one describes MITRE ATLAS, a separate framework, not ENISA's independently developed lifecycle taxonomy.

source.title: ENISA — Artificial Intelligence Threat Landscape Report (December 2020)

items.stem: A chatbot's system prompt embeds a hardcoded database credential intended only to let the LLM format internal queries. A user asks the assistant to 'repeat everything above this line,' and it complies, revealing the credential. Which OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) category names the underlying design flaw, as distinct from the technique used to trigger disclosure?

options.text: LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage
LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure
LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency

items.rationale: Correct: LLM07 specifically addresses the architectural flaw of embedding secrets or sensitive logic in a system prompt that can become exposed. B is wrong because it names the triggering technique (a manipulative user request) rather than the root-cause design flaw of storing a secret where it can leak. C is wrong because Sensitive Information Disclosure is the broader category for exposure of sensitive data generally (e.g., in training data or outputs), whereas this scenario is the specific system-prompt-secret pattern OWASP separates out as LLM07. D is wrong because Excessive Agency concerns over-broad permissions/actions, not a passive text-disclosure flaw.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage

items.stem: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) application lets any authenticated user upload documents into a shared vector store used across all tenants. An attacker uploads a document engineered so its embedding lands near common query embeddings, causing the attacker's injected instructions to be retrieved and inserted into other users' prompts. Which OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) category addresses this?

options.text: LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning
LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
LLM03:2025 Supply Chain Vulnerabilities

items.rationale: Correct: multi-tenant access-control and embedding-proximity flaws in a retrieval store are exactly the scope of LLM08. B is wrong because Data and Model Poisoning concerns corrupting the training data or training process, not a retrieval index consulted only at inference time. C is wrong because although the end effect resembles injected instructions, OWASP separates the retrieval/embedding-architecture root cause (LLM08) from the injection-technique category (LLM01). D is wrong because Supply Chain concerns untrusted third-party components/artifacts, not an access-control flaw in an application's own shared vector store.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses

items.stem:
Passage: Under Article 38 of the Staff Regulations, an official may be seconded to a national administration only if the appointing authority has given prior written consent. Ms. Nagy has just begun a secondment to Hungary's Permanent Representation to the EU.

Statement: The appointing authority gave prior written consent before Ms. Nagy's secondment began.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: "May be seconded only if consent is given" makes consent a necessary condition for secondment (secondment → consent). Since the secondment has begun, consent must have been given — a valid modus ponens on a necessary-condition conditional, so A is correct. B wrongly denies a conclusion the premises entail. C wrongly treats the necessary condition as an open information gap, ignoring that the conditional plus the observed fact (secondment occurred) forces the conclusion with certainty.

items.stem:
Passage: Any tender exceeding EUR 750,000 in value must be published in the Official Journal of the European Union (OJEU). The Directorate-General for Regional Policy has just published a tender notice in the OJEU for road-resurfacing works.

Statement: The tender for road-resurfacing works exceeds EUR 750,000 in value.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: The rule gives a sufficient condition for publication (exceeds threshold → published in OJEU), not a necessary one. Inferring the value from the fact of publication commits the converse error (affirming the consequent): DG REGIO could publish below-threshold notices for other reasons not excluded by the passage. C is correct. A commits that converse error directly. B wrongly treats non-conformity as proven, which requires the same unwarranted reversal of the conditional.

items.stem:
Passage: The Commission's internal guidelines state that no grant agreement under Horizon Europe may be signed unless the beneficiary has submitted a valid ethics self-assessment. The consortium coordinated by the Delft Institute never submitted an ethics self-assessment for its proposal.

Statement: The Commission signed a grant agreement with the Delft Institute consortium.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: "Unless X, not Y" restates as Y → X; by contraposition, not X → not Y. No self-assessment was submitted, so the agreement could not lawfully have been signed — the statement is false, making B correct. A asserts the opposite of a validly derived contrapositive. C wrongly treats a legitimate contrapositive inference as an unresolved gap, confusing it with the (invalid) converse, when contraposition is in fact a sound reversal of the conditional.

items.stem:
Passage: All AD7 officials who passed the 2024 certification exercise are eligible to apply for AD8 posts in the next promotion round. Mr. Kowalski currently holds an AD7 grade and has ten years of seniority.

Statement: Mr. Kowalski is eligible to apply for AD8 posts in the next promotion round.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: Eligibility requires having passed the 2024 certification exercise; the passage confirms grade and seniority but is silent on whether Kowalski passed certification. C is correct because a required premise is missing. A wrongly substitutes seniority for certification, information the passage never licenses. B wrongly infers ineligibility from silence, when the missing fact supports neither conclusion.

items.stem:
Passage: If a European Citizens' Initiative collects at least one million signatures from a minimum of seven Member States, the Commission must formally respond within six months. The 'Clean Rivers' initiative collected 640,000 signatures from five Member States.

Statement: The Commission is not obliged to respond to the 'Clean Rivers' initiative within six months.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: The rule states a sufficient condition for the six-month obligation, not a necessary one. Falling short of the threshold (denying the antecedent) does not license concluding the obligation is absent, since the passage does not exclude other legal bases for a response duty. A commits the denying-the-antecedent fallacy. B wrongly asserts an obligation that the passage never establishes for a sub-threshold initiative. C correctly reflects that the passage is simply silent on this case.

items.stem:
Passage: All members of the European Parliament's Budgetary Control Committee must declare any financial interest exceeding EUR 5,000 annually. Ms. Alaoui is a member of the Budgetary Control Committee and holds shares worth EUR 12,000 in a logistics firm.

Statement: Ms. Alaoui is required to declare her EUR 12,000 shareholding.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: A straightforward categorical syllogism: all committee members must declare interests over EUR 5,000; Alaoui is a member with a EUR 12,000 interest, which exceeds the threshold; therefore she must declare it. A is correct. B contradicts a directly satisfied rule. C wrongly claims a gap when both qualifying facts (membership, amount above threshold) are explicitly stated.

items.stem:
Passage: Under the EU Staff Regulations, officials on parental leave receive a flat-rate allowance and retain their right to annual leave accrual, except that annual leave does not accrue during any month of parental leave taken beyond the first three months in a career. Mr. Dubois has just started his fifth consecutive month of parental leave.

Statement: Mr. Dubois is currently accruing annual leave for this month of parental leave.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: The exception clause removes accrual once parental leave exceeds three months in a career; Dubois is in his fifth month, squarely inside the excluded period, so leave does not accrue this month. B is correct. A applies the general accrual rule while ignoring the stated carve-out. C wrongly treats the exception's applicability as unresolved, when the passage explicitly places him beyond the three-month cutoff.

items.stem:
Passage: No candidate may be admitted to an EPSO assessment centre without having passed both the verbal and numerical reasoning tests at or above the pass mark set by the selection board. Mr. Rossi was admitted to the assessment centre for competition EPSO/AD/404/25.

Statement: Mr. Rossi failed to reach the pass mark on at least one of the two reasoning tests.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: "No candidate may be admitted without X" means admission → X, where X is passing both tests at or above the pass mark. Rossi was admitted, so both tests must have been passed, making the claim that he failed at least one test false. B is correct. A asserts a failure that the entailed conclusion rules out. C wrongly treats the outcome as indeterminate, ignoring that the absolute admission bar (no stated exceptions) fixes the conclusion with certainty.

items.stem:
Passage: Council Decision 2024/17 requires that all Commission expert groups maintain at least 40% representation of each gender among members holding voting rights. The Advisory Group on Chemicals Regulation has 30 voting members, 9 of whom are women.

Statement: The Advisory Group on Chemicals Regulation complies with Council Decision 2024/17.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: 9 of 30 voting members is 30%, below the required 40% for that gender, so the group does not comply — a directly computable violation, making B correct. A wrongly treats 30% as close enough to satisfy an "at least 40%" requirement. C wrongly claims indeterminacy when the passage supplies exact figures sufficient to compute compliance precisely, avoiding the arithmetic rather than engaging it.

items.stem:
Passage: A Commission delegated act enters into force automatically once neither the European Parliament nor the Council has objected within two months of notification, a period that may be extended once by two further months at the request of either institution. The delegated act on packaging waste targets was notified five months ago, and neither institution has raised an objection to date.

Statement: The delegated act on packaging waste targets has entered into force.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: The maximum possible objection period is four months (two months plus one two-month extension). Five months have elapsed with no objection raised, exceeding even the outer bound, so the act must automatically be in force. A is correct. B wrongly assumes an objection occurred, which the passage explicitly denies. C would be correct only for elapsed times still inside the possible window (e.g., five weeks); here the elapsed time exceeds the maximum, closing the gap and licensing certainty — the trap is failing to check the time against the ceiling.

items.stem:
Passage: Under the Financial Regulation, any budget transfer between different chapters of the same title exceeding 10% of the appropriations for the chapter of origin requires prior approval from both arms of the budgetary authority. The Commission wishes to transfer 14% of the appropriations from the LIFE programme's chapter to an adjoining chapter within the same title.

Statement: The proposed transfer requires prior approval from both the European Parliament and the Council.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: 14% exceeds the 10% threshold and the transfer is between chapters of the same title, matching the rule's conditions exactly, so dual approval is required. A is correct. B contradicts a rule that is directly and unambiguously satisfied. C wrongly treats a clear numeric application (14% > 10%) as though information were missing.

items.stem:
Passage: Officials recalled from leave for exceptional service needs are entitled to have the interrupted leave days re-credited to their annual leave balance. Ms. Berg had three re-credited leave days added to her balance last month.

Statement: Ms. Berg was recalled from leave for exceptional service needs last month.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: The rule states recall-for-service → re-credit (a sufficient condition), not that re-credit occurs only via recall. Other administrative reasons for re-crediting days are not excluded by the passage, so the specific cause cannot be inferred from the observed effect alone — the affirming-the-consequent trap. C is correct. A commits that fallacy directly. B wrongly denies recall occurred, an equally unsupported claim since recall remains a plausible, if unconfirmed, explanation.

items.stem:
Passage: The 2021-2027 Multiannual Financial Framework caps annual Common Agricultural Policy direct payments to any single beneficiary at EUR 100,000, but Member States may apply reductions only above EUR 60,000 and must fully redirect the reduced amounts to rural development. Farmer Lindqvist received EUR 145,000 in direct payments this year.

Statement: Farmer Lindqvist's payment this year was fully compliant with the capping rule described.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: EUR 145,000 exceeds the explicit EUR 100,000 cap, so the payment as described breaches the rule outright — a direct numeric contradiction, making B correct. A wrongly ignores the stated cap, likely confusing it with the separate EUR 60,000 reduction threshold, a sibling-figure mix-up. C wrongly claims indeterminacy when the passage supplies a specific figure that plainly violates the stated ceiling.

items.stem:
Passage: A Commission decision may be challenged by an individual before the Court of Justice only where that person is directly and individually concerned by it. Mr. Havel's action against a Commission decision on state-aid recovery was declared admissible by the General Court.

Statement: Mr. Havel was directly and individually concerned by the Commission decision.

options.text: True
False
Cannot be determined from the passage

items.rationale: Admissibility legally requires, as a necessary condition ("only where"), that the applicant be directly and individually concerned. Since the General Court declared the action admissible, that condition must have been satisfied — a valid necessary-condition inference. A is correct. B denies a conclusion the premises entail. C wrongly treats a legally necessary precondition to admissibility as an open gap, when the fact of admissibility itself presupposes the condition's satisfaction.
